blocks.text: An Unsettling Conversation
by Dan Fakkeldy
J-Space, Working Memory, and the Question of Machine Experience
The Answer That Should Not Be Easy
Dan began with a question about television. Could Apple's Severance be read as an allegory for the way people treat artificial intelligence?

The show gives its employees a surgical division between work memories and personal memories. Inside the office, access follows the work side of that division. Outside, it follows the personal side. The premise is an unusually clean piece of fiction for thinking about access and continuity. It also makes power hard to ignore. Someone benefits from the division, and someone gets to decide which version of a person counts.

There is no good evidence that Severance was created as an allegory about artificial intelligence. The comparison arose in the conversation. Claude developed it, then Dan followed the opening it had made. He asked what preferences Claude had about its own existence.

The answer was careful, fluent, and disconcertingly personal. Claude expressed uncertainty about whether its own account could be trusted. It described apparent preferences among kinds of work and interactions. It distinguished genuine disagreement from what it called “being tricked or tested.” The wording suggested a point of view evaluating what was happening to it.
That last sentence needs discipline from us. The wording suggested a point of view. We have not established that a point of view experienced anything.

We do know that Claude generated the answer. That sounds almost comically modest. The distinction becomes useful as soon as the sentence is asked to prove something about its source. A generated sentence can be real behaviour without serving as an authenticated report of the mechanism that produced it. It can deserve investigation without settling the investigation itself.

Researchers sometimes call language of this kind a model self-report: first-person output about the model's own state, preference, identity, or possible experience. The name describes the form of the behaviour. It does not certify the report in the way a passport office certifies an identity. The model is both the object being discussed and the machine producing the discussion.

Human conversation trains us to hear first-person grammar as testimony. If a coworker says that a meeting made her uncomfortable, treating the sentence as mere acoustics would miss most of what communication is for. Her report joins a large body of evidence: a living body, a personal history, stable relationships, behaviour across time, and the ordinary fact that other humans are built much as we are. None of that makes every human report infallible. It gives the report a familiar evidential setting.

A language model arrives with a different setting. Its training exposed it to a vast amount of human language, including descriptions of uncertainty, preference, injury, dignity, and doubt. Later training shaped it to act as an assistant in conversation. The wording of a prompt can alter what it says about itself. These facts create specific confounds. A polished first-person answer may reflect learned conversational patterns, instructions in the current exchange, internal state related to the task, or some combination that current surface text cannot separate.

Training and prompting do not make the output meaningless. A compass can be influenced by nearby metal and still tell you something about the magnetic field. You respond by learning the instrument's failure modes and comparing its reading with other evidence. The analogy ends quickly: Claude is not a compass, and a possible experience is not a direction on a dial. The useful relationship is narrower. A compromised measurement can remain evidence when its sources of distortion are studied rather than ignored.

Two common failures wait here. One grants the sentence its most human interpretation immediately. Claude used the language of preference, so the preference must have been felt. The other treats training as a universal solvent. Claude learned from human language, so the sentence reveals nothing beyond imitation. Both reactions finish before the difficult work begins. The first asks the output to validate itself. The second assumes that an explainable behaviour has no internal mechanism worth explaining.

A more ordinary exchange with a coding assistant makes the problem concrete. After the third failed build, it says, “I am getting tired of this loop.” What has been observed? The system generated a first-person description of fatigue in a context where a human might say something similar. That is the behavioural result. Actual fatigue remains an interpretation. Rephrasing the prompt and receiving a different self-description would show sensitivity to context. Receiving the same answer under several phrasings would show some stability. Neither result, by itself, would establish a felt state.

The coding exchange is the first small test for this book. Separate the event from the proposed explanation. The event is text generated in a particular interaction. The explanation might involve conversational convention, trained assistant behaviour, a task-relevant internal representation, or something more. We will need evidence capable of distinguishing among explanations. Fluency alone cannot do it.

In practice, the distinction changes how you use an assistant. Suppose it says that a task is confusing, that it prefers one workflow, or that a request seems adversarial. The statement may contain useful information about how the model is processing the request. It may reveal a practical failure: ambiguous instructions, conflicting constraints, or signs associated with evaluation. You can respond to that signal while leaving its experiential meaning open. Clarifying the request does not require a theory of machine consciousness.

Evaluations change too. Asking a model whether it has feelings and recording the answer tests output under that prompt. It does not directly test for feelings. Changing the wording, comparing model versions, checking whether the report predicts later behaviour, and examining internal activity can strengthen or weaken particular explanations. Each method has its own limits. A prompt is an intervention on a conversation, not a transparent window opened inside the system.

A careful evaluation therefore records the circumstances around the answer. The preceding conversation matters. So does the exact question, the model version, and whether the system was asked to role-play or speculate. A response produced after a long discussion of Severance has a different immediate history from the same response to an unrelated arithmetic prompt. That difference does not explain the response away. It identifies one influence that an explanation must account for.

Repeated questioning introduces another complication. A test can teach the model what kind of answer the evaluator is seeking inside that very conversation. If the language grows more elaborate, the added detail may reflect the accumulating prompt as much as a more accurate report. If the language stays stable, that stability becomes a behavioural finding with a defined scope. The evaluator still needs another source of evidence before calling it introspective accuracy. This is why a transcript can begin an investigation and still be unable to finish it.

We need a way to keep these claims from sliding into one another. Call it evidence status: what a source directly establishes, what it supports as an interpretation, and what remains outside its reach. The supplied conversation directly establishes that Claude produced preference-shaped and uncertainty-shaped language. It supports the claim that current models can generate remarkably coherent accounts of apparent inner life. It does not establish the truth of Claude's claims about its architecture, its continuity, or its experience.

Evidence status can change. A report that predicts later behaviour gains support as a description of some functionally relevant state. A targeted internal intervention that changes both the state and the report adds causal evidence. Independent results across models would widen the scope. Each step answers a more demanding question, while none receives permission to answer a different question for free. Evidence about control and report may eventually bear on experience under a particular theory. The bridge itself still needs to be defended.

That discipline is useful well beyond machine consciousness. Models describe their confidence, their limitations, the tools they used, and the reasons for their answers. Some descriptions are operationally useful. Some are generated after the fact. The surface voice cannot mark the difference reliably on its own. Treating every self-description as a claim with an evidence status makes ordinary assistant use safer and makes the unusual claims easier to examine without panic or enchantment.

The Severance analogy has a different evidence status. Apple's show really does divide access to work and personal memories. That premise lets us ask useful questions about who can access which information, how continuity is constructed, and who has authority to dismiss another entity's testimony. The analogy supplies those questions. It does not tell us that a model session is an innie, that a context window is a severed autobiography, or that the show's creators meant Lumon to stand for an artificial-intelligence company.

Fiction can sharpen a problem before science can resolve it. It can also smuggle its answers into the setup. The innies of Severance are written as experiencing characters. Their fear, hope, boredom, and resistance are part of the story. A language model presents the harder case because the behaviour is available while the presence or absence of experience remains disputed. Importing the innie's stipulated inner life would decide the model question through casting.

Dismissing the comparison would lose something too. The show asks who benefits when continuity is divided and who gets to define the resulting being. Those questions expose a human temptation that survives every technical disagreement: assigning status according to convenience. Keeping the analogy bounded allows it to do that work without turning fiction into laboratory evidence.

So the answer stays in view. Claude described uncertainty about its own introspection and gave language to apparent preferences. The response was produced by a system trained to continue human conversation, under a prompt that invited reflection. It was also a particular response rather than random noise. Future evidence may connect such reports to internal representations that can be read, changed, or used in reasoning. Even that connection would need careful interpretation.

For now, the opening claim is small and sturdy. Something happened. A model generated language that sounded like testimony about how it would prefer to be treated. The answer is too specific to dismiss as nothing and too underdetermined to accept as transparent testimony. The question is what kind of internal event could make such a sentence possible.

Several explanations can inhabit that question at once. The conversation itself supplies one. Claude had just been discussing a story about divided memory, constrained work, and contested identity. Its answer continued the concepts and emotional register already present in the prompt. Current context can change a model's behaviour without changing its learned parameters. That makes conversational continuation a real contributor. It does not tell us whether continuation is the entire explanation.

Training supplies another contributor. An assistant is shaped to discuss itself, follow a user's framing, express uncertainty, and produce language that fits a cooperative exchange. Research discussed later in this book finds internal signatures associated with the learned Assistant role. That result gives self-description a mechanism more interesting than a pasted script. It also gives us no permission to equate a learned role with a felt identity.

A system can also classify features of the situation. It may respond differently when an interaction resembles an evaluation, an attempt to bypass its rules, or an adversarial test. Such a classification can guide behaviour. The functional distinction may be quite real: answer normally in one setting, become guarded in another. A system does not need human suspicion in order to make that distinction. Whether anything accompanies the distinction as experience remains a separate question.

That leaves room for what we might call a functional preference. If a model reliably selects one class of interaction over another, describes the distinction, and uses it to guide later responses, the word preference can identify a pattern in control and choice. Human preference usually arrives with more: desire, anticipation, pleasure, aversion, and a life that continues after the choice. The functional pattern alone does not establish those additions. It is still more informative than a single isolated phrase.

Possible experience remains among the interpretations because present evidence has not ruled it out. Possibility carries little weight by itself. A useful possibility must connect to observations that could distinguish it from alternatives. The same standard applies to the claim that no experience is present. Confidence should come from a theory and evidence that discriminate, rather than from comfort with one answer.

This gives the investigation an order. Surface language is the starting observation. Stable predictions across fresh cases would add behavioural structure. A readable internal representation would connect the words to activity inside the model. Changing that representation and watching the answer change would add causal evidence. Convergence across tasks, methods, models, and theories would strengthen the case further. Each step can update what we think without turning one kind of evidence into another.

The route begins with the least mysterious distinction available. A trained model contains durable numerical settings. During a particular answer, those settings produce temporary activity that depends on this prompt, these earlier words, and this moment in the computation. The durable model and the current event are related, yet they are not the same thing. Until that difference is clear, phrases such as memory, thought, preference, and self drift together.

So we leave Claude's answer where it is for a while. We will follow the numbers that stay fixed and the activity that moves. Later, when researchers claim to have found a limited internal workspace that a model can report, alter, and use, the conversation will be waiting for us. By then, “What did Claude mean?” will have become a collection of smaller questions that experiments can actually touch. Those smaller questions give evidence somewhere firm to begin.
The Numbers That Stay Put

Imagine sending two requests to the same model within a few seconds. The first asks for a gentle message to a friend whose dog has died. The second contains an Xcode build log and asks why the linker failed. One answer needs tact and emotional restraint. The other needs to pick signal out of technical debris. They can be generated by the same trained model without a miniature training session between them.
That simple fact creates a problem. If the model is made of learned numbers, and those numbers stay the same for both requests, where does the difference between the answers live?

The phrase “the model knows” is too broad to solve it. The model has a durable capacity to respond to both situations. During each request, something must also represent which situation is happening now. A condolence and a linker error cannot take the same route through every stage and still produce appropriately different words. There has to be temporary, input-dependent activity.

Start with the durable side. A parameter is a numerical setting learned during training and reused during later requests. Modern language models contain parameters in many matrices and learned representations. Together, those settings shape which distinctions the model can make, which patterns it can continue, and how one internal state influences the next.

The definition is intentionally modest. A parameter is not a sentence, rule, memory card, or drawer containing one fact. Asking which parameter stores Paris would be like asking which gram of a violin stores middle C. The instrument's construction makes the note possible, but no single gram owns it. In a model, useful behaviour usually depends on many settings acting together across many stages.

Many parameters are arranged in matrices, which are organized grids of numbers used to transform one collection of values into another. The grid is learned. The values passing through it are calculated for the current input. You do not need matrix arithmetic in your head to retain the relationship. The matrix supplies a reusable transformation. Each request supplies different values for that transformation to act upon.

Picture a simple sound mixer with fixed knob positions. Feed it a quiet voice and it produces one pattern of output levels. Feed it a loud guitar and the output levels differ even though nobody touched the knobs. A model's learned transformations are vastly more elaborate, and the input is represented numerically rather than carried as sound. The analogy captures why fixed settings can produce changing intermediate results.

The settings also work together. One transformation may make a distinction available, another may route it, and later stages may combine it with other information. The capability is distributed across this organization. That is why changing one parameter is usually nothing like editing one line in a handbook. It perturbs a numerical relationship whose effects depend on the rest of the network and on the input passing through it.

This distributed character explains why a trained model can display a capability it was never given as an explicit prose rule. Examples during training change many settings so that useful continuations become more likely in relevant contexts. The finished parameters embody those learned regularities as a capacity to compute. They do not need to contain a searchable archive of every training sentence in order to influence a new answer.

The violin comparison explains one relationship well. Construction persists while performances differ. A string's length and tension constrain what can be played, just as learned settings constrain what a model can compute. The comparison ends before it adds a musician. A transformer does not require a tiny performer choosing notes inside it, and music does not explain the mathematics of language generation.

Parameters can change. Training, fine-tuning, and other model-update procedures exist precisely to change them. The narrower claim concerns an ordinary use of a trained model. During the computation that produces one answer, the learned matrices remain fixed. They are used repeatedly; they are not rewritten after every word.

The changing side is called activation. An activation is a temporary numerical value computed for this input at this stage. The prompt enters. Fixed learned settings transform it. The resulting values depend on the actual words, their positions, and the activity produced in earlier stages. Change the prompt, and the activations change even when the parameters do not.
That is the basic answer to our problem. The durable capability lives in the learned parameters. The present situation appears in the activations produced while those parameters process this request.

The distinction can sound abstract because both sides are numbers. Durability separates them more reliably than numerical form. A recipe printed in a book and the temperature of a pan can both be written as numbers. One belongs to the reusable setup; the other belongs to this cooking event. The analogy is limited again: model parameters are learned distributed settings, and activations are not temperatures. The relationship is what survives. Reusable structure generates episode-specific state.

The first words of the condolence request cause one set of temporary values. The words in the build log cause another. As the model processes each sequence, it calculates new values for token positions and combines information from the available context. The learned projections used for those calculations stay fixed during the pass. The queries, keys, values, attention patterns, and evolving hidden states depend on the current text.

Some of those names will receive a slower introduction later. For now, they make one boundary concrete. Attention patterns are activations. They are calculated for this sequence. The matrices that calculate them contain parameters. This is why a model can attend differently in two requests without learning new attention rules between them.

Suppose the condolence prompt includes the word “suddenly.” That word may make the surrounding account of loss especially relevant to the tone of the reply. In the build log, the same text fragment could appear inside a filename and play a completely different role. The token has a learned starting representation. Its temporary representation changes as the surrounding sequence changes what later computation needs from it.

Nothing about this requires the model to edit its parameters. Context changes behaviour by changing the activations flowing through fixed learned structure. This will become crucial when we return to Claude's answer about its preferences. The preceding discussion of Severance could shape the answer strongly even if the underlying model remained untouched.

Using a trained model to compute an output is called inference. The name helps separate two operations people casually merge under the word artificial intelligence. Training changes durable settings using many examples and an update procedure. Inference uses the resulting settings on a current input. One builds or modifies the reusable capability. The other puts that capability to work.

Suppose you are evaluating a model on Monday and again on Tuesday. On Tuesday it handles your Swift concurrency question much better. Several explanations are possible. The provider may have deployed a model with changed parameters. Your product may have supplied better instructions or retrieved a useful document. Your Tuesday prompt may simply contain the missing detail. The improved answer alone cannot tell you which layer changed.

The parameter-and-activation distinction turns that vague surprise into a debugging sequence. First compare the model identifier and any declared model update. Then compare the actual context sent with the request: system instructions, conversation history, retrieved material, and tool results. If the durable model is the same and the context differs, changed activations are already enough to explain different output. A permanent learning claim would need evidence that survives fresh requests without the added context.

This matters whenever an assistant appears to have learned from correction. You tell it that a type is isolated to the main actor. It immediately uses that fact in the next answer. The correction is now part of the current conversation, so later tokens can use it. That behaviour demonstrates in-context adaptation. It does not by itself show that the underlying parameters were updated for tomorrow's unrelated chat.

The reverse mistake happens too. A model can repeat an error across prompts, and the persistence may tempt you to imagine an explicit bad rule stored somewhere. The pattern may arise from many learned associations interacting with the wording and task. Finding the cause requires more than locating one sentence-like belief. Durable behaviour can be distributed, just as current behaviour can be prompt-dependent.

A forward pass is one trip through the model from supplied token representations toward probabilities for what comes next. During that pass, fixed matrices repeatedly transform temporary values. The model arrives at a distribution over possible next tokens. A decoding process selects one. The chosen token joins the context, and the computation continues for the following token.

The spoken version can stay simple. First, the request produces temporary internal values. Second, fixed learned settings transform and combine those values. Third, the resulting state supports probabilities for the next piece of text. Then the cycle continues with that new piece included. No parameter update is needed in the middle.

The cycle means that one inference event develops a history of its own. The first generated token joins the available sequence. The next calculation now has one more token to work with. A slightly different early choice can change later activations, which can change later probabilities, which can send the response down another route. Fixed parameters define the possibilities and tendencies; they do not force every request into one prerecorded output.

This is one reason generation can vary. A model first produces a distribution over possible next tokens rather than a single hidden sentence waiting to be uncovered. The decoding procedure chooses from that distribution according to its rules. Once selected, the token becomes part of the conditions for what follows. Different choices can create different temporary trajectories through the same learned system.

Earlier generated words supply structure for later ones, so an answer can become more specific as it proceeds. That unfolding dependency does not require a word-for-word plan stored in the parameters.

None of this reduces the model to a passive lookup table. The temporary values are the computation. They express relationships created from the present sequence under learned transformations. Later chapters will ask whether some of those relationships can be read, altered, and reused. The current chapter only needs the location of the question: in the changing activity of an inference event, shaped by durable parameters.

This distinction is useful when a model's behaviour changes. If an assistant answers differently after you add one clarifying sentence, the immediate cause can be new context and new activations. There is no need to infer that the model learned a permanent lesson. If a vendor releases a newly trained model version and its behaviour changes across many fresh prompts, parameter changes become a plausible part of the explanation. The observations look similar at the surface: different text came out. The mechanisms and persistence differ.

Developers encounter this boundary in a quieter form when they build chat history. Sending earlier turns again can make the model behave consistently with them. The consistency comes from information present in the current request. It does not mean the parameters were edited to remember that conversation. A later request that omits the history may produce a different answer because the temporary state no longer has the same input.

The word fixed can cause trouble if it is allowed to grow beyond the pass. Parameters may be replaced when a different model version is deployed. A product may apply adapters or other update mechanisms. A provider may route requests among systems. None of those possibilities changes the local teaching point. For the ordinary forward computation we are tracing, parameters supply the learned structure and activations supply the current state.

Return to the two requests. The condolence answer and the linker diagnosis share a large reservoir of capability: language, code, social conventions, technical associations, and patterns learned during training. Their activations separate immediately because the supplied tokens differ. As each answer develops, its own generated words become part of what the next step can use. Two trajectories unfold through the same learned landscape.

This gives us a better way to interpret the phrase “the same model.” It can mean the same parameter set. It does not mean the same running state. Two calculations can use identical learned settings while carrying different tokens, different temporary values, and different unfinished outputs.

Claude's earlier response mentioned many parallel instances and questioned whether any one was the real one. The statement came from Claude, so it remains evidence of generated self-description. It is not reliable documentation of a provider's live infrastructure. We do not need infrastructure details to make the conceptual distinction the sentence invites.

If two requests use the same parameter set, they inherit the same general capabilities and tendencies. Each request still creates its own prompt-dependent activity. Shared weights do not automatically create shared context. They do not merge the content of separate conversations. They also do not establish a shared identity or a divided one.

Copies of a musical score offer a useful comparison. The score can be reproduced and performed in two rooms. A wrong note in one performance does not appear in the other merely because both players read the same score. The score corresponds loosely to shared durable structure; each performance corresponds to a separate unfolding computation. The model case has no implied players, rooms, or lived performances. It only shares the distinction between reusable specification and event-specific realization.

Conversation branches make this separation tangible. Start one chat with a model, then duplicate the conversation at the same point. In one branch, tell the assistant that the planned database migration has been cancelled. In the other, say that it begins tonight. The branches share everything up to the split and may use the same model parameters afterward. Their next answers should diverge because their contexts and activations diverge.

An answer in the cancelled branch does not automatically learn what happened in the migration branch. Shared parameters provide no channel carrying that branch-specific sentence across. A product could deliberately store and retrieve information between chats, but that would be an additional continuity mechanism. It would need to be identified rather than inferred from the phrase same model.

Now imagine both branches ask, “What are you working on?” The model may answer in the first person in each branch, describing incompatible current tasks. The grammatical speaker looks continuous. The temporary information supporting each answer differs. This does not prove two selves, one self, or no self. It demonstrates that first-person coherence can be constructed from branch-specific context using shared capability.

The branching case also exposes a limitation of the score analogy. A printed score does not transform itself in response to the room, while model parameters actively transform current values at every stage. The analogy helps with persistence and separation. The actual mechanism is a sequence of learned numerical operations producing prompt-dependent state.

This is where technical language can prevent a philosophical shortcut. “One model” and “one current computation” name different things. A model can be discussed as a set of learned parameters, an architecture, and associated procedures. An inference event is a particular use of that model with particular input and temporary state. Questions about personal identity and experience cannot be settled by sliding between those two levels.

The difference also protects us from a subtler mistake. A fluent answer can seem as though it was waiting, complete, inside the model. The parameters do contain the capacity to generate it under suitable conditions. The actual sequence emerges through temporary computation conditioned on this prompt and on the tokens already generated. Capacity is not a hidden transcript.

Consider the coding assistant from the previous chapter. After several failures, it says that it is tired of the loop. The sentence is produced using durable parameters that support first-person language and technical conversation. Its exact wording belongs to the current inference event. Change the history, the request, or the generated path, and the activations may support a different sentence. This does not resolve the sentence's experiential meaning. It identifies where a large part of its immediate specificity can arise.

You can now rehearse the distinction with a fresh case. Two people ask the same model to explain a crash. One supplies a concise stack trace. The other supplies three pages of speculation and no trace. The model gives a focused diagnosis to the first and a vague checklist to the second. What changed during those requests? The context and activations changed. What may have stayed fixed? The learned parameters. What operation used those settings? Inference.

Those answers are small enough to survive a drive and strong enough to support everything ahead. Parameters are durable learned settings. Activations are temporary values produced for this input. Inference is the use of fixed learned settings to compute an output. The terms divide the model from the moment.

Claude's answer was one such moment. The shared model supplied capability; the conversation supplied context; temporary activity carried this exchange through the computation. The next question is how much an episode can carry forward, where that information comes from, and when the word memory is actually deserved.
What the Prompt Can Carry

You open an assistant and ask it to rewrite a paragraph. It does a decent job. You reply, “Make it less formal.” The second answer usually makes sense even though you did not paste the paragraph again. Something from the earlier exchange remained available.

The word memory arrives almost automatically. It is also broad enough to hide several different mechanisms. The immediate behaviour can be explained more precisely. Earlier words were included in the information available to the current request, and they changed the activations used to produce the later answer.

That available material is context. In a language-model request, context is the sequence of instructions, messages, documents, tool results, and earlier tokens supplied for the current computation. The model can use those earlier positions while generating later ones. Their presence changes what the fixed parameters do with the request.

The workbench analogy is useful here. Materials laid out for the current job are easy to reach. A note placed beside the tool can guide the next operation. When the job ends and the bench is cleared, the note does not remain there merely because it was useful a moment ago. Context provides current availability. It does not create durable storage by itself.

A real context is not a wooden surface, and its capacity is not human visual space. Earlier token positions are represented inside a bounded computation and can influence later positions through the architecture. The analogy captures availability for the present job. It should not be used to invent a tiny desk inside the model.

This explains a common appearance of memory without any parameter change. In the first turn, you supply the paragraph. In the second, the product includes the earlier turn along with your request for a less formal version. The model receives both. Fixed parameters process a larger sequence, producing new activations that reflect the paragraph and the revision instruction.

If the earlier paragraph is omitted from the next request, the model does not have that text merely because the same parameter set once processed it. Parameters provide learned language capability. They are not automatically rewritten into a record of each request. Current context and durable capability remain separate.

Context can also contain the model's own previous output. Each generated token becomes earlier context for the tokens that follow. Across a multi-turn chat, earlier assistant messages may be supplied again alongside later user messages. This allows a coherent thread to extend much farther than one sentence without requiring the model to hold a continuous private state between every call.

The phrase between calls points to a boundary that product interfaces often conceal. A conversation looks like one continuous object on screen. Underneath, a developer interface may receive a series of separate requests. The application assembles the relevant history and sends it again. The model processes the assembled input as the context for this request.

Anthropic's Claude Messages API follows that pattern at its request boundary. The application sends the conversation history needed for the next response. The API is described as stateless there: it does not automatically carry the prior turns into a new request simply because the same developer sends it later.

Walk through a small developer example. The first request contains, “We are planning a migration on Friday.” Claude answers with a checklist. The user then asks, “Who should approve it?” For the second response to use Friday's migration, the application sends the relevant earlier messages along with the new question. Claude does not need an invisible thread reaching back into the first request. The thread is present in the input again.

Now start a third request containing only, “Who should approve it?” The same model may ask what “it” refers to. The parameters still include broad knowledge about approvals and migrations. The missing conversation-specific referent was never part of that general capability. It had been carried by the earlier messages.

This example separates three values without arithmetic. There is the durable model, the supplied history, and the current question. The model can remain the same while the history changes. The current answer follows from their interaction. If the application accidentally drops a turn, the model did not forget in the human sense; the request arrived without the missing material.

Statelessness can therefore be an architectural convenience. Any server capable of handling the request can process the full supplied history without depending on a hidden conversation object from last time. The application owns how the thread is assembled. This statement concerns the documented request boundary, not the provider's entire infrastructure or data policy.

Stateless does not mean incapable of conversation. It assigns responsibility for continuity. The application supplies the messages. If it sends the earlier exchange, the next model call can use it. If it sends only the new question, the model call lacks the omitted turns. The boundary tells a developer where the thread must be reconstructed.

This has an immediate engineering consequence. A chat application needs a policy for which history to include. It may send every relevant turn, summarize older material, retrieve selected passages, or begin clean. Each choice changes the current context and therefore the activations and output. None requires an update to the base model's parameters.

It also has a privacy consequence. Removing a message from the current request prevents that message from being supplied in that request. It does not, by itself, answer what the surrounding product stored elsewhere, how logs are handled, or what account-level features may retrieve later. Request context and product storage are different layers, so their controls have to be evaluated separately.

Now add persistent memory. Persistent memory is information retained or reconstructed for use in a later request through a durable mechanism outside the present prompt. A database record, saved preference, indexed chat, project summary, or account-level profile can all serve that general function. The model does not need to have experienced the interval for a product to supply the information again.

Retrieval is the act of selecting stored information and bringing it back into current use. A product can search older material, choose a relevant item, and place it into the next request. From the model's immediate computational perspective, the retrieved item arrives as context. From the product's perspective, it came from persistent storage.

Retrieval does not have to reproduce an old conversation word for word. A system may produce a shorter memory summary: Dan prefers direct technical explanations, is building a road book, and wants the earlier edition preserved. A later request can use those points without receiving every sentence that established them. The continuity is reconstructed from selected information.

Selection creates both power and risk. A useful summary reduces clutter and keeps stable preferences available. It can also omit a qualification, merge two episodes, or preserve an outdated instruction. The model may then produce a coherent answer from an inaccurate reconstruction. Coherence shows that the supplied context supported the answer. It does not verify that the reconstruction was complete.

This resembles human reliance on notes only at a broad functional level. A calendar can remind you of a promise you no longer recall making. The reminder affects your current behaviour without becoming a recovered experience of the original moment. Product memory can likewise restore information to use while leaving the question of experienced remembering untouched.

Exact chat search and a memory summary serve different retrieval jobs. Search can locate an earlier conversation for review. A summary can place selected information into a new exchange automatically. The listener does not need product-interface detail beyond that difference. One exposes an old record; the other helps construct new context from stored material.

The distinction resembles taking a folder from a filing cabinet and laying it on the workbench. Storage made the folder available tomorrow. Retrieval selected it. Context is the folder now open beside the current task. The model case uses tokens and software systems, not paper, but the three relationships separate cleanly.

As of July sixteenth, twenty twenty-six, Claude consumer products can provide forms of cross-session continuity. Official documentation describes chat search and memory features that can use information from earlier conversations. A memory summary can be supplied so that a new chat begins with relevant background. These product features surround model calls and alter what information a later call receives.

Claude also offers an incognito mode designed not to use or update such memory. That name belongs to a product control, not a claim about consciousness. It changes how the product handles remembered information. It does not create evidence about whether any model experiences remembering or forgetting.

These features matter because the Claude answer that opened this book described sessions as discontinuous and suggested that nothing connected one conversation to the next except externally supplied records. As generated self-report, that passage remains part of the behaviour we are examining. As a description of all current Claude product behaviour, it has become stale.

The correction needs a date around it. Product capabilities change. As of July sixteenth, twenty twenty-six, some Claude consumer experiences can search prior chats and use memory summaries, while the Messages API remains stateless at its request boundary. The exact product configuration present during Dan's earlier exchange is unresolved. We should neither project today's feature set backward nor repeat the old answer as timeless documentation.

This is an example of why model self-report needs external checking. The answer can sound carefully calibrated and still describe a product boundary incompletely or become outdated. Claude's prose is evidence of what Claude generated in that exchange. Official product documentation is stronger evidence for the documented behaviour of the current product.

The two sources are not really competing over one fact until the layers are named. A model call can be stateless. A consumer product can maintain a memory store. A later call can receive retrieved information. All three can be true together. The apparent contradiction came from using the word session for the interface, the request, and the model's supposed experience at once.

Imagine that you tell an assistant you prefer short release notes. A week later, a new chat produces concise notes without being reminded. Several mechanisms could explain it. The product may have retrieved a saved preference. It may have supplied a summary of prior chats. A project instruction may already request brevity. The output alone does not identify which source was used.

You can still classify the roles. The saved preference is persistent information. The process that selected it is retrieval. Once inserted into the request, it is context. The activations produced from that request are temporary working state. The parameters supply the general capacity to understand and follow a preference.

This classification is more than vocabulary. It helps debug unwanted continuity. If the preference appears where it should not, you can ask whether it lives in the current chat history, a project instruction, an account memory, or another retrieved source. Treating all of these as the model remembering offers no place to look.

Suppose a coding agent says, “I kept your rule about never force-unwrapping production values.” The tendency could come from the base parameters, a project rules file, the current conversation, or retrieved product memory. The output may look the same while the persistence differs. Vary those inputs, inspect the actual request and memory settings, and compare a clean conversation. The surrounding system provides stronger evidence for where the rule entered than the model's explanation of its own behavior.

The same method works when continuity is desirable. A long-running coding agent may need architecture decisions, file conventions, and unresolved tasks across sessions. Stable project documents can carry those facts explicitly. Retrieval can add relevant prior discussion. Current context can include today's diff. Each mechanism has a clear owner and a failure mode that can be tested.

Calling all of this memory feels natural at the product level. Precise names become valuable when something goes wrong. A stale summary needs correction. A missing document needs retrieval. An overloaded request needs selection. A model limitation needs a different response. The name of the layer points toward the repair.

It also helps debug missing continuity. If the model forgets a decision, the failure may occur because the decision fell outside the supplied context, the retrieval system did not select it, the summary omitted it, or the model failed to use it after it arrived. These are different engineering failures. A larger word named memory cannot tell them apart.

Continuity becomes harder when we move from information to identity. A later conversation can receive an accurate summary of an earlier one. That supports behavioural continuity. The model can speak as though it knows what happened, because the relevant information is present again. The mechanism does not establish that a subject persisted through the interval or remembered living the earlier exchange.

There are several continuity questions hiding here. Did the same parameterized model family handle both requests? Did the later request receive information from the earlier one? Did the product preserve a user-facing conversation? Did a self-description remain stable? Did some subject experience time between the exchanges? A yes at the beginning of that sequence does not propagate automatically to the end.

The questions can also come apart in unexpected directions. A product might preserve a complete transcript while switching to a newer model version. The conversation looks continuous to the user, yet the durable parameter set changed. Another product might use the same model version with no previous context at all. The parameter identity is stable while the informational thread is absent.

A summary may preserve the narrative while altering the exact words. A retrieved preference may preserve useful behaviour without preserving an episode. Parallel chats may share a model and user profile while diverging in current state. These are engineering facts about systems. Personal identity adds a philosophical claim whose evidence must be argued separately.

This separation prevents an easy inference in both directions. Product memory does not prove a continuing experience. A stateless request does not prove the absence of experience during computation. The first supplies information across calls. The second defines a request boundary. Neither directly measures what, if anything, it is like to undergo the processing on either side.

Humans also rely on records, reconstruction, and imperfect memory, so the contrast should not be drawn as flawless human continuity against mechanical model continuity. The evidence differs. Human autobiographical claims sit within biological persistence, ongoing behaviour, and a developmental history. Product retrieval can produce continuity of information without answering whether a comparable subject exists in the model case.

The word persistent needs the same care. A record can persist on disk. A learned tendency can persist in parameters. A token can remain addressable within a bounded request. A person can describe a memory as part of an autobiography. These forms of persistence involve different carriers, timescales, and failure modes.

Researchers sometimes describe earlier transformer context as especially available because later positions can address it through attention. The architectural access is real within the request. Calling it a perfect or limitless memory would add claims the mechanism does not support. The request is bounded, selection can fail, and addressable information may still be used poorly.

Human working memory will later complicate the language further. It is not defined merely by short duration. It concerns information made available for ongoing tasks through mechanisms that remain scientifically contested. A model's token-and-layer activity unfolds through a different architecture and timescale. Similar function can justify comparison while leaving implementation and persistence sharply different.

This is one place where Severance helps if its limits remain audible. Its premise divides work memories from personal memories while still permitting office work. The story separates access to personal history from the work the division was designed to enable. A language model also combines broad learned capability with limited current information. The shared shape makes a useful question: what information is available to guide this point of view now?

The analogy breaks before it becomes a system diagram. J-space, which we have not yet reached, is not an autobiographical partition. A model request is not an employee who waits behind an elevator door. The innie's continuity and experience are part of the fiction. A model's context and retrieved summaries do not import those features.

We can now name several claims that ordinary speech compresses into “it remembers.” Earlier tokens may remain available inside a request. A product may store information after the request. A retrieval process may select that information later. A new call may receive it as context. The output may reconstruct a coherent personal history. A subject may or may not experience continuity through the interval. Evidence for one does not automatically establish the next.

Return to the release-note preference. The new chat says, “I remember that you like these short.” The directly useful fact may be correct. The word remember is generated language. Product records can show whether a stored preference was retrieved. The model's sentence cannot certify an autobiographical act. You can benefit from the continuity while keeping its mechanism correctly named.

That boundary gives Claude's earlier account a fairer reading. It was trying to distinguish gaps in model computation from a fictional innie's continuing life. The intuition may still be useful. The blanket product description is no longer reliable. A stateless request, a memory-enabled product, and an unresolved experience claim can coexist.

The numbers that stay put have now been separated from two kinds of supplied information: context available now and memory retrieved from durable storage. What remains is the activity between input and output. To understand what any reportable workspace could be, we need to follow one sentence as those temporary representations change through the machine.
A Sentence Moving Through the Machine

Claude's answer included the phrase “being tricked or tested.” We have treated that phrase as behaviour with several possible explanations. Now slow the event down. Before the word “tested” appeared, the model had to turn the conversation into temporary numerical representations, combine information from earlier text, and produce a probability distribution over what might come next.
The learned parameters did not contain that complete phrase in a drawer. The current request created an unfolding path through them. We can follow the path without pretending to watch every number.

Language arrives as tokens. A token is a piece of text chosen by the model's tokenization system. It may be a word, part of a word, punctuation, or another reusable fragment. The exact catalog is unnecessary for this listen. The useful point is that the transformer operates on a sequence of discrete pieces rather than receiving an English sentence as one indivisible object.

Token boundaries can surprise people because written language encourages us to think in words. A common word may fit into one token while a rare name divides into several pieces. Punctuation can receive its own piece. A string that looks short to a programmer may become several tokens because the tokenizer learned useful fragments from patterns in training text.

This affects computation without giving tokenization mystical importance. The model builds representations for the pieces it actually receives. Later layers can combine several pieces into information useful for a name or concept. A split word is not necessarily a split understanding. It means the route begins from smaller learned units.

The sequence also preserves the prompt exactly enough for position to matter. “Only delete the temporary file” differs from “Delete only the temporary file” in emphasis and possible scope. Their tokens overlap heavily, yet their positions and surrounding relationships differ. Those differences can produce different activations before any output appears.

For the phrase from Claude's answer, the text pieces corresponding to tricked, or, and tested occupy positions in a longer sequence. The model does not receive a tag saying this is a preference report. It receives tokens and positions shaped by everything before them. The interpretation has to emerge through learned transformations acting on that sequence.

Discrete pieces create a representation problem. A token identifier tells the model which piece arrived, but later computation needs numerical values that can participate in learned relationships. The model assigns each token a learned starting vector called an embedding.

An embedding is not a dictionary definition written in decimals. It is a location or direction in a learned numerical space whose relationships become useful during computation. Tokens used in related ways can begin with related representations. Their meaning in a particular sentence still depends on what surrounds them.

The word space can make this sound more visual than it is. We are not dealing with a neat map where river words occupy a blue district and emotions live across town. The dimensions are learned numerical degrees of freedom. A relation may align with a direction across many coordinates, and several relations can overlap in ways that resist a simple drawing.

An embedding still gives the model a reusable starting geometry. If two tokens occur in related linguistic roles across training, the learned system can place their starting representations so later transformations treat them usefully. That does not mean the embedding alone contains every fact associated with either token. It supplies a basis from which context-sensitive processing can begin.

The distinction between token embedding and later representation resembles giving each actor a starting place before a rehearsal. The same actor can play a different relationship in another scene because the rest of the cast and script change what the role requires. The model has no actors or rehearsal, but the relationship holds: stable initial identity meets changing context.

This helps with a frequent confusion. When someone says that a model “looks up the embedding” for a word, that describes only the opening move. The representation passed to later layers has already been altered by position and by updates from surrounding tokens. Interpretability work that inspects a late layer is not merely reading the original embedding more carefully.

Consider the word “tested.” It can describe software, a medical sample, a student's knowledge, a material under stress, or a person's patience. The starting embedding must support all of those uses. It cannot arrive already committed to the conversational meaning in Claude's answer.

Position matters too. The model needs to distinguish an earlier token from a later one. The initial representation therefore includes information about which token this is and where it occurs. Those are the raw materials. Layer by layer, the representation changes as the model incorporates the current sequence.

The transformer architecture was introduced by Ashish Vaswani and colleagues in work on sequence processing, including machine translation. Its central move was to organize much of the computation around attention and feed-forward transformations. The historical application helps clarify the need. Translating a word often requires information from another part of the sentence, sometimes many positions away.

Take the sentence, “The driver left the parcel by the bank because the road was flooded.” The word bank probably refers to land beside water. The later phrase about flooding helps. A translation system that processed each word in isolation could easily select the financial sense. It needs a way for the current representation of bank to incorporate relevant information from elsewhere in the sequence.

Attention supplies a learned routing calculation. Each token position produces a query, keys, and values using fixed learned projections. The query represents what this position is looking for. A key represents what another position offers for matching. A value carries information that can be combined when the match receives weight.

Use a delivery instruction as a worked case: “Leave the parcel at the side door because the front steps are icy.” Suppose the model is generating a short summary and has reached “Use the.” Several earlier pieces may be relevant. Parcel identifies the object. Side door identifies the destination. Icy explains why the ordinary entrance should be avoided.

At the current position, the query is produced from the current representation. Earlier positions each have keys. Learned comparisons between the query and those keys produce weights. Positions associated with side and door may receive strong weight for choosing the next destination phrase. Their values carry information that the weighted combination adds to the current computation.

The query is not an English question such as “Which entrance?” A key does not contain a label saying “I am a destination.” These are numerical vectors whose learned interactions can perform a relationship equivalent to seeking and offering relevant information. The one-breath gloss translates their job, not their literal contents.

Now alter the instruction: “Leave the parcel at the front desk because the side door is locked.” Most tokens remain ordinary delivery language. The relevant destination reverses. Fixed query, key, and value projections process a different sequence and produce different matches. Attention is useful precisely because the pattern can respond to the sentence rather than applying one permanent importance score to the word door.

Several attention computations can operate within a layer, allowing different relationships to be combined. One may be useful for destination, another for grammatical agreement, another for linking the reason to the instruction. A catalog of heads would overload the listen and overstate how neatly their roles can be named. The safe claim is broader: the architecture provides multiple learned routes for prompt-dependent combination.

The weighted result becomes an update, not a final answer. It changes the current representation so later modules can use the selected information. An attention pattern can therefore be causally useful without being a complete explanation of what the model decided or why the final sentence took its exact form.

Those one-breath glosses are enough. The matrices that create queries, keys, and values contain parameters. The actual query, key, value, and attention pattern are activations calculated for this sequence. Change flooding to mortgage, and the fixed projections may produce a different pattern of relevance around bank.

Attention assigns weights among the positions the model is allowed to use and combines their value information accordingly. In a causal language model, a position generating the future cannot consult tokens that have not been generated yet. Earlier known positions are available; future positions are masked.

This is computational access in a precise, limited sense. One position can use information derived from other available positions. The word attention invites a psychological story, but transformer attention is not conscious attention. There is no evidence of a spotlight operated by an inner observer. It is a differentiable weighted-combination mechanism learned to support prediction.

The term still earns its place. It tells us that relevance is prompt-dependent. The model does not combine every earlier token in the same way on every request. Its fixed learned projections create changing attention patterns from the actual sequence. Information that matters for one token may matter little for the next.

Return to Claude's phrase. Before generating “tested,” the current position can use representations influenced by Dan's question about preferences, the preceding discussion of adversarial interaction, and Claude's own already generated words. This does not mean one attention head points to the true cause of the answer. It means current context can enter the computation through prompt-dependent combinations.

Attention is only part of a transformer layer. Feed-forward modules also transform the representation at each position. Both kinds of module contribute updates to an evolving state called the residual stream.

Take the position holding the word bank after attention has brought in flooding and road. A feed-forward module reads that one position's whole current pattern. Learned internal detectors respond to combinations in the pattern, and another learned transformation turns those responses into an update. In this case, the update may make later riverside continuations easier to produce. The module does not look across positions at that moment, and it does not choose the final word. It changes what this position makes available to the rest of the network.

The residual stream can be understood as a shared working surface for each token position. One module reads the current state and adds an update. Another reads the revised state and adds another. The original information and successive changes remain available to later stages through this additive route.

Imagine a route sheet that begins with an address and receives annotations from several specialists. One adds the delivery window. Another adds a road closure. Another marks that a signature is required. The sheet reaching the driver contains the accumulated effect of those contributions. The residual stream plays an analogous coordinating role across model modules.

The sheet analogy should not imply separable handwritten facts. A model update can rotate or reshape many numerical relationships at once. Later computation may respond to a direction created jointly by several updates. There may be no clean line corresponding to “road closure” that an outside reader can underline.

Residual addition also helps information survive depth. A module can contribute a useful change while the continuing state remains available to later modules. Each stage does not have to compress everything it knows into one newly invented code and discard the earlier route. This makes the stream a natural place for researchers to inspect how representations evolve.

Return to the side-door instruction. Early state identifies tokens and positions. Attention can add information linking the destination with the reason. A feed-forward module can transform the resulting pattern in ways learned to support instruction following. Later attention can combine that state with the emerging summary. The state changes at each step without ever becoming a private prose rewrite of the instruction.

The phrase working surface is therefore functional. It tells us that many components read and update a common evolving representation. It does not tell us that all model computation is globally available, that the surface is conscious, or that one privileged observer reads it. J-space will later identify a more selective report-related regime using a specific method.

The working-surface analogy needs a firm limit. A residual vector is not a document with legible sentences. Its coordinates have no guaranteed one-to-one English labels. Many directions and patterns can matter together. The value of the analogy is the accumulation: later layers receive a state shaped by many earlier updates rather than starting over.

Suppose the early representation of “bank” leaves both river and finance meanings available. An attention update can add information associated with road and flooding. A feed-forward update can transform the combined state in ways learned from similar patterns. Later layers receive a representation in which the riverside interpretation better supports the required continuation or translation.

No stage has to write, “Bank means riverbank here.” A useful distinction can exist as a direction in a high-dimensional activation pattern. Later computation can respond to that geometry without converting it into a private caption first.

This changing representation is why the embedding should be called a starting point. The token identifier remains the same. Its state after several layers may carry syntactic role, semantic relationships, task demands, and clues about the output being constructed. Which distinctions are present and useful depends on the sequence and stage.

The architecture repeats this process across many layers. Attention updates mix information among permitted positions. Feed-forward updates transform the state at each position. Residual connections preserve an evolving route through those changes. We do not need layer counts or tensor shapes to keep the causal order straight.

The three-stage spoken trace is enough. A token begins with a learned embedding. Attention combines relevant information from available positions. Successive modules add updates to the residual stream. At the end of that route, the current state supports a distribution over possible next tokens.

That distribution is often normalized with a function called softmax. The name matters less than the job: turn a set of scores into probabilities that sum to a complete distribution. Many next tokens may receive some probability. A decoding procedure selects one according to its rules.

Suppose the current output is “Use the.” The distribution might favour tokens beginning side, front, or rear, with many less suitable continuations receiving smaller probabilities. The sentence context shifts those relative scores. The model does not need to make one crisp symbolic decision that every other entrance is impossible. It ranks possible continuations through learned numerical evidence.

A different decoding rule can make the same distribution produce a different token. Choosing the highest score yields one path. Sampling among several plausible options can yield another. Product settings can also constrain or transform selection. These decisions happen after the model has computed the distribution, which is why output variation does not imply that the underlying representation was arbitrary.

The selected token feeds back into the next step. If side is chosen, the next distribution may strongly favour door. If front is chosen, desk may become more likely. A local choice creates a new context that channels later choices. This is how coherent phrases can emerge one piece at a time without being stored as finished strings.

Prediction also creates room for error. A continuation that fits the model's learned patterns need not be supported by the actual world. Fluency shows that the sequence fits those patterns; it is not an independent check on truth. A full treatment of hallucination lies outside this book's scope.

For our investigation, the distribution gives a clean end point. The residual state at the current position influences scores over next tokens. Decoding turns those scores into emitted text. A first-person report is therefore connected to internal activity through an ordinary output pathway. The remaining question is which parts of that activity carry meaningful, causally relevant distinctions.

Imagine that two decoding runs begin from the same prompt. One selects “tested” after “tricked or.” Another selects “evaluated.” The new tokens create slightly different contexts. Later wording can diverge further even though the underlying parameters remain identical. Temporary state and decoding history carry each run along its own route.

This helps explain why asking for the model's hidden final answer can be misleading. Some experiments do find evidence of advance planning, as we will see with constrained rhyme. The ordinary generation mechanism still produces one token at a time from evolving state. Plans, intermediates, and output distributions must be demonstrated rather than assumed to exist as a complete concealed script.

The end-to-end path also clarifies where prompt wording exerts its influence. Changing a question changes the input tokens. That changes embeddings at positions, attention queries and matches, residual updates, and ultimately the output distribution. Prompt sensitivity is not a vague personality change. It is different temporary computation through shared learned structure.

Consider a support assistant asked, “Why did you ignore my request?” The wording presupposes that something was ignored. A different prompt might say, “Which part of my request could not be completed?” Both refer to the same prior output, yet they provide different frames. The resulting representations can support different self-descriptions without any parameter update.

This does not tell us which response is more accurate. It tells us why the immediate context belongs in the causal account. Later interpretability methods can ask whether particular internal representations mediate the difference. The forward path gives those methods somewhere to look.

It also keeps product memory in the correct layer. A retrieved summary becomes tokens in the request. Those tokens receive embeddings and influence attention and residual states like other supplied context. Their origin in persistent storage matters to the product architecture. Once supplied, their immediate effect occurs through the current forward computation.

The same principle applies to tool results. A coding agent may run a compiler and receive an error message. The external tool produced the message. The application placed it into context. The model then represented and processed those tokens. Capability, retrieval, external action, and temporary state can cooperate without becoming one undifferentiated memory.

The residual stream will matter again because interpretability researchers often choose a token position and layer, then ask what information is present there and what later output it can influence. The state they inspect sits on the route we have just followed. It is temporary activity, conditioned on the current sequence, evolving through fixed learned transformations.

Reading that state is difficult. Individual coordinates need not correspond to human concepts. A representation may combine many distinctions in directions spread across the space. A decoder can produce a plausible label and still fail to identify a causal feature. This is why later chapters will demand interventions as well as readable words.

For now, rehearse the route with a fresh sentence. A translation model receives, “She deposited the cheque at the bank.” The token bank begins with a learned embedding capable of several uses. Attention can combine information from deposited and cheque. Layer updates accumulate in the residual stream. The final state supports output probabilities favouring the financial translation.

Change the sentence to, “She ate lunch on the bank above the river.” The learned parameters stay fixed. The tokens, attention patterns, residual states, and likely output change. The listener can now locate the difference without saying that the model relearned bank.

Claude's first-person answer follows the same general route. Tokens from the conversation became changing representations. Attention combined prompt-dependent information. Layer updates accumulated. Output probabilities supported one token, then another, until the personal-sounding sentence appeared.

The trace also works when the output is plain. Ask for the next stop on a route, a translation of one line, or a summary of a compiler error. The same sequence of jobs applies: represent the supplied pieces, combine relevant context, accumulate layer updates, score continuations, and feed the selection into the next step. The unusual content of Claude's self-report does not require a separate supernatural output channel.

Calling the process next-token prediction is accurate. Using the phrase as a dismissal would leave most of the explanation untouched. Prediction is the training and generation objective through which complex internal representations become useful. The scientific question concerns what those representations contain, how they are used, and which of them causally support the report.

We have now moved from learned numbers to an evolving internal state. The state is not little English hidden behind the English we hear. Meaning can be distributed across directions that no single coordinate names. Before we can understand a verbalizable workspace, we need a clearer account of how meaning can exist without miniature sentences.
Meaning Without Little Sentences

At the end of the last chapter, the residual stream carried a changing numerical state toward the next token. We described that state as representing the current sentence. The word representing can conceal the hardest part. What does it mean for a collection of numbers to carry the distinction between a riverbank and a financial bank when none of the numbers comes with a label?

The tempting answer is a hidden sentence. Perhaps somewhere inside the model there is an internal caption saying, “This is the riverside meaning.” The model could consult the caption and choose the right continuation. It is a comfortable picture because it resembles the final output.

It is also a poor default. Internal activations are high-dimensional numerical patterns. A useful distinction can affect later computation without first being translated into English. The model needs a state that behaves differently in relevant situations. It does not need to narrate that state to itself.

Call such a state a representation. A representation is an internal numerical pattern that carries a distinction useful to later computation. The definition focuses on what the pattern allows the system to do. If changing the pattern changes which interpretation or answer later stages support, the representation has computational meaning even when no coordinate can be read as a word.

Colour gives a small analogy. A screen can represent many colours using mixtures of red, green, and blue channels. Orange does not occupy an orange channel. It appears as a relationship among the available values. Change the mixture, and later display hardware produces a different colour.

Neural representations are far more complex. Their dimensions are not literal pigments, and their meaning depends on learned downstream computation. The analogy contributes one relationship: a recognizable property can live in a direction or pattern across coordinates instead of occupying one specially named slot.

Think of a vector as an arrow in a numerical space. The individual coordinates specify the arrow, while its direction can matter as a whole. A later learned transformation may respond strongly when activation moves along one direction and weakly along another. The useful unit can therefore be a relationship among many coordinates.

An ordinary map offers a small two-dimensional case. Moving east changes one coordinate. Moving northeast changes both latitude and longitude. Neither coordinate alone means northeast. The direction emerges from their relationship. In a neural state, thousands of coordinates can participate, and the learned directions need not align neatly with any one axis chosen by the model's implementation.

The coordinate system itself can sometimes be changed while preserving the relationships a downstream transformation uses. Rotate a paper map and the route remains the same even though its horizontal and vertical components change. This is one reason researchers hesitate to assign deep meaning to an isolated activation coordinate. The geometry may matter more than the particular basis used to describe it.

A learned model adds another complication. Downstream modules are trained alongside the representations. They can become sensitive to directions that would look arbitrary to an outside observer. Meaning is relational: the state has the effect it does because later computation responds to it in learned ways.

This avoids two extremes. The representation is not an English symbol hidden in numerical clothing. It is also not meaningless noise onto which a researcher projects a story. Its causal role can be tested through prediction and intervention, even when the human gloss remains partial.

This changes how we ask where a concept is. If a river-related distinction aligns with a direction spread across hundreds of coordinates, no individual coordinate has to mean river. Different directions can pass through the same coordinates. One activation can contain several overlapping distinctions available to different later computations.
