Researchers often use the word feature for an operationally meaningful direction or pattern they can test. The operational part matters. A method identifies a candidate feature because it predicts, decodes, or causally influences behaviour under defined conditions. Naming it does not prove that the model has one natural, unique concept matching the researcher's label.

Suppose a decoder finds a direction that separates formal from informal language in a collection of prompts. The direction may be useful. It can help predict tone or provide a target for intervention. Its label summarizes what the tested examples have in common. The same direction might also correlate with politeness, genre, or prompt source. More tests are needed before the label becomes a clean causal explanation.

This is why a readable word from an interpretability tool needs a boundary around it. The label is an outside description of a pattern. It is not necessarily a word stored inside the model. A direction detected by this method is safer language than the model's concept when uniqueness and scope have not been established.

Distributed representation also helps explain contextual meaning. The starting embedding for bank is reusable. Attention and feed-forward updates move the current activation according to the sentence. The riverside use and financial use can occupy different regions or directions in later activity even though they began from the same token embedding.

The model does not have to erase one definition and paste in another. It can transform a broad starting representation into a context-specific state whose geometry changes what later layers and the output map do. Meaning is expressed in the consequences of that state across the remaining computation.

Now consider how many distinctions a model may need. Tone, syntax, entities, spatial relations, code structure, factual associations, task goals, and unfinished plans can all matter during one request. A simple picture gives each distinction its own independent coordinate. Real systems may need to represent more useful features than an obvious one-slot-per-feature arrangement would allow.

Toy models demonstrate one possible response called superposition. Superposition encodes multiple useful features in non-orthogonal directions. The directions overlap in the available dimensions instead of receiving perfectly separate axes.

The phrase more features than dimensions can sound impossible if a feature is imagined as a drawer. Directions are not drawers. Many arrows can be drawn through a room even though the room has only three spatial dimensions. They interfere with one another more than perfectly perpendicular axes would, but a system can still make useful tradeoffs when features are sparse or do not all activate together.

A shared radio medium offers another relationship analogy. Several signals can occupy one physical medium when their structure allows a receiver to separate them. The analogy ends before it reaches the mechanism. Neural superposition is not frequency tuning, and a transformer does not contain little stations broadcasting concepts. Both cases show that sharing coordinates need not collapse every distinction into noise.

Toy-model evidence is valuable because it proves that learned systems can choose such geometry under defined pressures. It does not prove that every frontier-model feature has a clean linear direction or that superposition explains every uninterpretable activation. A simplified demonstration establishes possibility and mechanism in the toy setting. Generality remains an empirical question.

Sparsity helps the toy mechanism. If only a few features are active in any one example, overlapping directions do not always collide. The system can devote clean independent dimensions to every feature, or it can tolerate occasional interference and represent a larger useful set. Training can favour the second arrangement when capacity is constrained and most combinations are rare.

Imagine a route assistant with features for apartment building, school zone, rural road, signature required, aggressive dog warning, and icy steps. A particular delivery may activate only two or three. Shared numerical dimensions can carry those sparse combinations more successfully than they could carry every feature at full strength at once.

When unusual combinations occur together, interference may create errors or ambiguous readouts. That is part of the explanation rather than an embarrassing exception. Superposition trades clean separation for representational capacity under certain conditions. The details in a real language model may be much more complicated, but the toy result shows why a one-coordinate-per-meaning expectation is unnecessary.

It also explains why interpretability methods often seek sparse decompositions. A method can try to recover a small set of active directions from a dense-looking state. Success can make behaviour easier to analyze. The recovered basis remains a modeling choice whose stability, completeness, and causal role require testing.

Common or important distinctions may receive cleaner representation while rare combinations tolerate more interference. The exact organization depends on training, architecture, data, and the tasks the system has learned to support.

This offers one explanation for why individual coordinates often resist interpretation. A coordinate can participate in many directions. Watching it rise may reflect several possible features or combinations. Its meaning changes with the rest of the activation pattern. A coordinate-level story can therefore be unstable even when direction-level structure is useful.

The same problem appears in reverse. A researcher can find multiple directions that predict similar behaviour. Sparse decompositions can choose one useful basis among several. Rotating or recombining directions may preserve much of the same information. The representation can be real and causally useful without granting one mathematically privileged vocabulary.

That nonuniqueness matters for J-space later. The paper identifies a sparse token-indexed decomposition of especially verbalizable activity. Sparse means that relatively few components are active in a given case under the method. It does not guarantee that those components are the only valid axes or that each corresponds to one indivisible natural concept.

We can see the difference between a representation and a coordinate with a delivery example. Suppose a route assistant must distinguish apartment deliveries from detached houses. No single coordinate needs to contain apartment. A pattern involving unit numbers, lobby access, buzzer instructions, and address format can move activity in a direction that later computation uses to suggest the right procedure.

Change the address from “12 King Street” to “12 King Street, unit 804.” The parameters remain fixed. The added tokens alter attention and residual updates. A building-type distinction may become stronger in the current representation. Later output can recommend a buzzer or lobby without storing a prose rule in one activation slot.

The representation is episode-specific. It exists in the activations produced for this address and prompt. The model's general capability to form such distinctions depends on durable parameters. If the request ends, that particular activation pattern does not become a permanent record merely because it carried meaning.

This corrects another common collapse. Representation is not the same as memory. A calculator temporarily represents the result of an addition while computing the next operation. The value can influence the calculation without being filed into a personal history. A model's activation can similarly carry task-relevant information without changing weights or creating cross-session storage.

The calculator analogy is deliberately plain. A language model's representations are richer, distributed, and shaped by context. The shared relationship is temporary availability for computation. Neither case needs autobiographical remembering for a value to be causally useful.

Context is also distinct from representation. Context is the supplied token sequence. Representation is the changing numerical state computed from that sequence. Two models with different parameters can receive identical context and form different representations. The same model can receive slightly different context and form different state.

Persistent memory adds another layer. A product may retrieve a saved address preference and place it into context. The forward pass turns the retrieved tokens into activations. The current representation can guide output. Storage, retrieval, supplied context, and representation all contribute, but they remain separable mechanisms.

These separations matter when an internal readout appears to reveal a thought. A decoder might map current activity to a word such as deception, test, or preference. The label can be useful evidence that the state contains a related distinction. It cannot tell us, by itself, whether the distinction came from current prompt wording, a broad task representation, a learned assistant role, or another correlated factor.

Nor can it tell us whether the represented distinction is available for flexible use. Information can exist in a system and influence one narrow pathway without being reportable or reusable elsewhere. Existence and access are different. The J-space experiments will test that difference through reporting, instruction-driven modulation, reuse, connectivity, and ablation.

A spelling checker illustrates the gap in a small way. It may represent that a token sequence violates a learned spelling pattern and use the distinction to rank a correction. That does not imply the system can answer a question about why the spelling is wrong, apply the distinction to a different task, or describe its own uncertainty. The information exists for one operation; broader access has not been shown.

Language models are more flexible, so the boundary needs experiments rather than assumptions. A decoded direction related to rhyme might guide word selection. Researchers can then ask whether the same content can be reported, suppressed on instruction, or reused in another part of the task. Each additional use supports a stronger claim about access.

The reverse can happen as well. A model may verbally report a concept because the prompt makes the word likely, while the decoded internal direction plays no causal role in the underlying task. Report and representation can align by correlation. Swapping or ablating the state helps test whether the alignment participates in the computation.

This hierarchy gives interpretable labels a productive place. A label proposes what a direction may carry. Behavioural tests ask where the proposal predicts output. Causal tests ask whether changing the direction changes the result. Flexible-use tests ask how broadly the content can be recruited. None of these steps requires pretending that the direction is a little sentence.

Before those experiments, we need a standard stronger than interpretation by label. If a direction is said to represent riverbank, alter it in a controlled setting and test whether river-related output changes. If a direction is said to carry an intermediate answer, swap it and see whether the final answer follows. Causal intervention can distinguish a state that helps produce behaviour from one that merely accompanies it.

Even causal results need careful scope. A successful intervention at one layer, token, prompt, and metric shows relevance under that setup. It does not prove that the direction is the only route, that the label captures everything it does, or that the representation is stable across models. Operational precision protects the useful result from an inflated interpretation.

Return to the phrase “being tricked or tested.” One possibility is that the current activation contains a distinction related to evaluation or adversarial interaction. That distinction may be distributed across directions shaped by the whole prompt. A later output map can turn the resulting state into first-person language.

The model does not need a hidden sentence saying, “I dislike this.” It needs internal activity that changes what it will report and how it will act. Whether that functional state is accompanied by experience is a further claim. Distributed representation can explain how the distinction exists computationally without deciding what the existence feels like, or whether it feels at all.

This is also why absence from a readable decoder cannot prove absence from the model. The method may miss a nonlinear, distributed, or weak representation. The chosen vocabulary may be wrong. The information may be present in a form inaccessible to the decoder. Negative interpretability results must be tied to the sensitivity and assumptions of the instrument.

Positive results have their own failure modes. A decoder can exploit a correlation useful for classification while ignoring the causal feature the researcher intended. It can read information that later model computation never uses. A crisp label can conceal those possibilities because language makes the result feel more settled than the method warrants.

The next chapter gives us the tools to push back. Activation patching will replace selected internal state from one run with state from another. Ablation will suppress or replace a candidate component. These interventions do not solve interpretation automatically, but they ask a stronger question: when this internal pattern changes, does the downstream behaviour change in the predicted way?

Rehearse the current chapter with a fresh case first. A model reads, “The crane lifted the steel beam.” The token crane begins with a representation compatible with a bird or a machine. Contextual updates move the current state toward construction machinery. The useful distinction can be a distributed direction. No coordinate needs to be labelled machine, and no hidden English sentence is required.

Now change the sentence to, “The crane spread its wings over the marsh.” The parameters remain fixed. Context changes the activations. A different direction becomes useful to later output. The model's capability persists; the represented case changes; no personal memory has been created.

Meaning inside a transformer can therefore be real without being sentence-shaped. It can be distributed without being arbitrary. It can be temporary without being causally inert. Those three facts make interpretability possible and prevent it from becoming an exercise in reading labels as revelations.

If an internal tool prints the label “evaluation,” the disciplined response now has three parts. Ask which activation direction produced the label. Ask what prompts and tasks support that gloss. Then ask whether changing the direction changes evaluation-related behaviour. The label begins a testable account; it does not complete one.

The next question is experimental. A candidate representation has been found. Reading it is the beginning. To learn whether it participates in the answer, researchers have to change the hidden state and watch what follows.
How to Push on a Hidden State

At the end of the last chapter, we had arrived at a promising internal pattern. Perhaps a decoder could read it as a distinction between a bird and a construction machine, or between an ordinary request and an evaluation. The pattern appeared at the right time. It predicted something about the answer. That is useful evidence, but it leaves a quiet problem behind.

The pattern might help produce the answer. It might instead reflect a decision already made elsewhere. It might be a side effect carried along by the same prompt features that drive the answer. All three possibilities can create a convincing correlation.

A dashboard warning light makes the problem concrete. When an engine overheats, a temperature light may turn on. The light predicts trouble and represents a real condition. Cutting the wire to the light will not cool the engine. The light is informative without being a cause of the heat.

The comparison has a limit. A neural activation is part of the same computation that produces later activity, while a dashboard light is designed mainly for a human observer. Some internal states genuinely transmit information used downstream. The warning light still gives us the right first question: have we found a participant in the process, or only a readable companion to it?

Researchers answer by changing the candidate state and measuring a defined consequence. This controlled change is an intervention. It turns an observation about what happened into a question about what would have happened if one part of the internal activity had been different.

That phrase, what would have happened, is the center of the method. A single model run gives one history. An intervention creates a carefully limited alternative. The parameters can stay fixed. The prompt can stay mostly fixed. The experimenter changes a selected activation and watches whether the downstream result moves in the predicted direction.

Imagine two nearly identical requests to a route assistant. In one, the delivery note says that a parcel requires a signature. In the other, it says that no signature is needed. The model produces different internal activity and, eventually, different instructions to the driver.

Suppose an interpretability method identifies a candidate direction associated with the signature requirement. The direction is stronger in the first run. A decoder can tell the two runs apart. So far, the result is correlational. The words in the prompt, many other activations, and the output all changed together.

A more revealing experiment keeps the no-signature request as the destination run and inserts a selected piece of internal activity from the signature-required source run. If the final output now becomes more likely to recommend obtaining a signature, the changed state has done more than accompany the original answer. Under this setup, it has helped redirect the computation.

This procedure is called activation patching. Activity from one run is patched into another at a selected location and moment. The researcher then compares the resulting output against a metric chosen in advance.

A metric is simply the defined change that counts as the predicted effect. It might compare the model's relative support for two possible answers. It might measure whether a particular conclusion becomes more or less likely. The metric turns “the output seemed different” into a specific test.

The two runs need careful names because several changes are involved. The source run supplies the activation. The destination run receives it. Researchers sometimes create the destination by corrupting part of the original input, where corruption means a controlled alteration for the experiment, not random damage to the whole model.

In the delivery example, the source carries the signature-required state. The destination begins from the no-signature request. The patch asks whether inserting the selected source activity into the destination can recover some of the source answer.

If it can, the experiment supports a counterfactual claim: with this prompt pair, at this intervention site, and according to this output measure, the source activity makes a causal difference. Counterfactual here means a controlled comparison with an alternative internal state.

This is stronger than discovering that a decoder can read the signature distinction. The model's own later computation responded to the inserted state. A human label was no longer the only bridge between activation and behavior.

The result remains bounded. The patch does not establish that this site is the only route by which the signature information travels. The model may encode the same distinction redundantly in several places. Other components may reconstruct it. The patch may also affect related features that the decoder's label does not capture.

The conclusion belongs to the tested task, prompt pair, location, intervention, and metric. A different request may use another route. A patch at another token or layer may behave differently. A successful local result earns a local causal claim.

For a software developer, activation patching resembles changing a value while a program is paused in a debugger. A function is about to calculate a delivery instruction. The developer replaces one variable and continues execution. If the result changes as expected, that variable mattered along the observed path.

The relationship is useful because both experiments inject state and inspect a downstream consequence. The neural case is less tidy. A debugger may expose a named variable with a declared type and a clear owner. A language model's information can be distributed across many coordinates, mixed with other distinctions, and transformed continuously as it moves forward.

There is also no guarantee that an injected neural state fits naturally with everything around it. An activation taken from one run carries relationships shaped by that run's context. Placing it into another context can create an unusual combination the model rarely encountered during training.

Researchers call such a combination off-manifold when it falls outside the familiar region of states produced by ordinary inputs. The phrase refers to the geometry of the activations, not to a literal surface inside the machine. An intervention that creates a highly unnatural state may cause a large output change for the wrong reason.

Careful experiments therefore use closely controlled runs and compare interventions. They look for predicted direction, selectivity, and consistency. A patch that moves only the intended output under several relevant variations is more persuasive than one that makes every answer unstable.

Location provides another comparison. A patch made early in the computation may transfer a broad interpretation of the request. A later patch may transfer only a conclusion already refined by many intervening steps. If both alter the answer, they can still be doing different work. The pattern across locations helps distinguish where information first becomes usable from where it is merely preserved for output.

Token position matters for the same reason. Activity associated with the word signature may be readable at the input token, while the state used to form an instruction may appear at the final token where the model is preparing its next word. A successful patch at one position does not automatically describe the role of the other. The forward pass is a sequence of changing representations, not one fixed picture stretched across the prompt.

Metrics can separate effects that fluent prose conceals. A patched answer might still sound natural while its preference between “obtain a signature” and “leave the parcel” changes sharply. Another patch might alter wording without changing the operational recommendation. Measuring the intended decision alongside general output quality helps tell targeted redirection from broad disturbance.

These comparisons make a causal map from many modest results. No single patch has to carry the entire explanation. Early and late sites, source and destination pairs, target and control measures, and nearby prompt variations can reveal a stable pattern of influence.

The source and destination also matter. If the prompts differ in many ways, their activations may carry many differences at once. Patching a broad state can transfer tone, topic, syntax, and task along with the feature of interest. A narrow experimental contrast makes the causal interpretation easier to defend.

This is why activation patching is an experiment design problem as much as an interpretability technique. The tool can replace a vector. The scientific work lies in choosing what to compare, where to intervene, what outcome to measure, and what alternative explanations to rule out.

The method also exposes a deeper fact about representation. An internal state can be meaningful because later computation is sensitive to it. When the state changes and the answer follows in a predictable way, meaning is connected to use. The human gloss may still be incomplete, but the state is no longer merely a shape that an outside decoder happens to recognize.

There are several possible outcomes. A patch may recover the expected answer strongly. It may create a small shift. It may have no measured effect. It may alter an unrelated part of the output. Each result changes the next question, though none carries a complete explanation by itself.

A weak effect could mean the selected state contributes only part of the necessary information. It could mean the intervention site is poorly chosen. The decoder may have read a correlate. The output measure may miss a change occurring elsewhere. The destination run may contain enough competing information to overwhelm the patch.

A null result is similarly ambiguous. The candidate representation may be causally irrelevant. It may also be duplicated, reconstructed later, or usable only together with other activity that was not transferred. A failed patch narrows a claim under the tested conditions; it does not prove that the information exists nowhere in the network.

Activation patching asks what happens when one run lends activity to another. A second family of experiments asks what happens when a candidate component is suppressed or replaced. This is called ablation.

The everyday image is removal. A researcher finds a component thought to matter and takes it away. In a neural network, taking away always requires some choice about what goes in its place. Setting activity to a neutral-looking value, substituting a baseline, or otherwise suppressing a direction all create altered systems with different assumptions.

There is no universally empty neural value that means “this thought is absent and nothing else changed.” A zero can be ordinary in one representation and unusual in another. A replacement based on typical activity may preserve some relationships while disrupting others. The result has to be interpreted relative to the replacement actually used.

Suppose the route assistant contains a candidate component associated with signature requirements. An ablation suppresses that component while leaving the prompt unchanged. If the model now fails selectively on signature instructions while retaining ordinary route guidance, the component appears necessary for that tested behavior under that replacement.

The word selectively carries weight. If the model's grammar collapses, its topic drifts, and every answer deteriorates, the experiment shows that a broad disruption occurred. It says less about the proposed signature function. A focused impairment fits the causal story better.

Even a focused impairment needs context. The component may carry a cluster of related distinctions rather than one clean concept. It may support a general operation used by the signature task. The label summarizes the observed role; further contrasts decide how narrow that role can become.

Ablation can also spare the behavior. That outcome does not automatically make the component irrelevant. Neural systems can have redundant paths. Later layers can compensate for missing input. The task may be easy enough for another cue in the prompt to carry the answer.

Think of closing a bridge in a city. Traffic may slow dramatically, revealing dependence on that crossing. It may divert onto another bridge and arrive only slightly later. It may remain almost unchanged at a quiet hour. The observed effect depends on the surrounding network and on how travelers adapt.

The bridge comparison also has a firm limit. Neural compensation does not imply conscious rerouting or a city planner inside the model. It refers to other learned pathways producing a similar downstream result when one pathway is disturbed.

An ablation creates a new system for the duration of the test. The modified network may operate in a region its training never prepared it for. Large damage can cascade. Small damage can be repaired by existing computation. Both possibilities make replacement controls and comparison tasks essential.

Patching and ablation therefore answer related questions from different directions. Patching asks whether inserted activity can redirect a result. Ablation asks whether behavior depends on a candidate component when that component is suppressed or replaced. Agreement between them is more informative than either result in isolation.

Imagine a sequence of findings. A decoder reads a signature-related direction. The direction appears when the prompt requires a signature. Patching it into the no-signature run shifts the output toward obtaining one. Suppressing it impairs signature instructions more than unrelated route guidance. The pieces begin to converge.

The decoder supplies a description. The patch supplies evidence of causal sufficiency in a limited counterfactual sense: adding the activity can help create the predicted effect. The ablation supplies evidence of task dependence in a limited necessity sense: removing or replacing it impairs the behavior under that setup.

Neither “sufficient” nor “necessary” should be heard without its boundary. The patch may be sufficient only alongside the destination run's remaining activity. The ablation may reveal necessity only because of the chosen replacement and prompt. Scientific shorthand is useful when its conditions travel with it.

Convergence can include more than these two techniques. Researchers can vary prompts, compare layers and token positions, use different readout methods, test alternative labels, and measure unrelated control tasks. A mechanism becomes more credible as independent probes make compatible predictions.

The controls can also expose an overconfident label. Suppose the direction called signature requirement changes outputs about permission, obligation, and confirmation more generally. The original name may have captured one visible case of a broader instruction-following feature. The causal finding survives, while the human description becomes less narrow. Interpretability improves when experiments are allowed to revise the vocabulary.

This gradual accumulation is especially important when the subject invites rich interpretation. A readable activation associated with planning, self-reference, evaluation, or experience can encourage a story larger than the experiment. Causal tests keep the story attached to observable changes.

They do not settle every interesting question. Showing that a state helps produce a self-report establishes a functional role in generating that report. It does not establish why the state formed, whether the same representation appears across models, or whether any subjective experience accompanies it.

The boundary is not a retreat from the result. It tells us what has genuinely been learned. The model contained activity that could be read in a particular way, and changing that activity changed a defined behavior. That is already a substantial advance over guessing from output alone.

This gives us an evidential standard for the strange internal space ahead. A method that merely prints plausible words would be interesting and vulnerable. Its labels might track prompt content without participating in the model's work.

The case becomes stronger if selected internal content can be swapped between runs and the model's report follows the swap. It becomes stronger again if instructions can encourage or suppress that content in a controlled way. Selective ablations can test which tasks depend on the candidate space and which survive without it.

Those experiments do not all ask the same thing. Readout asks what an outside instrument can recover. A report swap asks whether inserted content can redirect what the model says. Instruction-driven modulation asks whether the model can recruit or withhold the content through its ordinary control mechanisms. Ablation asks what changes when the candidate space is disrupted.

Together they approach a richer idea: some internal information may be available for flexible use across several operations. A representation that can be read, deliberately modulated, reused in reasoning, and causally connected to later output has earned more than a catchy label.

The phrase earned more remains deliberately open. Flexible functional access is not the same claim as consciousness. A space can coordinate report and reasoning without answering whether there is anything it is like to occupy that space. The later philosophy depends on keeping those questions apart.

For now, the experimental ladder is enough. Correlation locates a candidate. Patching tests a controlled alternative. Ablation tests dependence under a chosen replacement. Variation and control tasks test the scope. Convergence supports a bounded mechanism.

Return once more to the model that said it disliked being tricked or tested. Output alone leaves many explanations. The phrase may be a learned conversational response, an inference about the user's scenario, a representation of adversarial interaction, or some combination of these.

If an instrument finds evaluation-related internal activity, that finding adds a second observation. If changing the activity predictably changes evaluation-related reports while preserving unrelated capabilities, the causal case grows. If the same content can guide hidden intermediate reasoning, respond to instruction, and support several tasks, the functional account becomes richer still.

At no point does the experiment need to declare the model sincere or insincere. Those are broad judgments about an entity and its report. The measurements ask narrower questions about information, access, control, and consequence.

Narrow questions can carry us surprisingly far. They can distinguish a phrase copied from the prompt from a state formed during computation. They can distinguish readable residue from activity used downstream. They can reveal where a report depends on a shared internal route and where automatic processing continues elsewhere.

The next instrument begins with an unusual idea. Instead of asking only which output the model will produce, it asks which small changes in internal activity would most directly change what the model could say. It is a lens built around possible speech.

That lens will reveal a sparse, token-indexed space the researchers call J-space. The name will matter less than the tests behind it. We now know what those tests must accomplish: the room has to earn its name through access, intervention, and selective dependence.
A Lens for What Could Be Said

The experiment now begins at one exact point in a model's forward pass. A prompt has been turned into tokens. Those tokens have become embeddings. Attention and feed-forward modules have made successive updates to the residual stream. At a chosen token and layer, the researcher pauses over one high-dimensional activation.

The state contains far more coordinates than a person could inspect by ear or eye. Its values are numerical. Its useful distinctions may be distributed. Even if a word such as evaluation would describe part of the activity well, no coordinate has to bear that label.

The practical question is narrower than translation. If this local state changed slightly, which words later in the model's output would become more likely? A good instrument would connect directions in the hidden activation to directions in possible speech.

One way to picture the problem is a car resting on a broad landscape. The car sits at one point. A small push north may send it gently downhill. A small push east may meet a steep rise. The slopes around the car summarize how nearby movements would change its height.

A model's hidden state has vastly more possible directions than a car on a landscape. Its downstream result is also richer than height. Still, a local slope can tell how a small change in one direction would affect a later quantity. In calculus, the collection of those sensitivities is called a Jacobian.

No derivation is needed for the main idea. The Jacobian records local responsiveness. It asks how a small change here is expected to alter something there. The answer is an approximation near the state where the measurement is made.

Consider a hypothetical prompt whose answer depends on the intermediate idea winter. The model has not yet produced that word. Somewhere along the forward pass, a current activation may help later computation favour cold-weather conclusions. An ordinary decoder asks whether winter can be predicted from the activation. The local-sensitivity question asks something different: which small direction of change here would most increase the model's later tendency to produce winter?

Those questions can agree, but they need not. A state can contain information that an outside decoder recovers even when later model computation ignores it. Another direction can have a strong downstream effect despite being difficult to classify from the state alone. The J-lens is designed around influence on possible verbal output.

The measurement begins with the residual activation at one token and layer. It considers how a small perturbation to that state would propagate toward a later output-relevant representation. The model's normal vocabulary map then translates that downstream direction into ranked token associations.

The word winter at the top of a readout therefore compresses a chain. A distributed local direction changes a later numerical state. The output map associates that changed state with greater support for the winter token. The printed word is the instrument's gloss on that sensitivity.

This does not require the model to be about to say winter. The word may name an intermediate useful for a later answer. It may be one member of a related cluster. It may never appear in the final response. The lens is aimed at what the state could help verbalize, not only at the next word already winning the output competition.

The researchers behind the J-space paper use that relationship to connect intermediate residual activity to later output-relevant representations. They average the local map over a collection of model activity, then apply the model's ordinary decoding machinery to rank token directions promoted by a hidden activation.
This instrument is the Jacobian lens, often shortened to J-lens. It estimates how local hidden-state directions would influence words the model could produce at the current or a later point.

The averaging matters. A Jacobian calculated for one exact prompt can be entangled with the peculiarities of that run. Averaging across a corpus seeks a more stable map between hidden directions and possible verbal output. The method trades some prompt-specific detail for a reusable readout.

The average acts like a calibration assembled from many journeys over similar terrain. A bump peculiar to one route matters less. Repeated slopes remain. This can make common transformations easier to read across prompts.

The same averaging can wash out rare or context-dependent routes. If a direction matters only when a particular phrase, language, or task is present, a broad average may weaken it. Stability and specificity pull in different directions, and the method chooses a point between them.

The resulting map is applied back to individual activations. Prompt-specific content enters through the current hidden state; the averaged Jacobian supplies the common local relationship to downstream verbal output. The readout combines a reusable instrument with a transient case.

The landscape analogy reaches its limit here. Terrain has a few familiar spatial directions and a fixed physical height. A transformer activation has many learned dimensions, and later computation can bend, mix, and gate their effects. The J-lens is a first-order approximation. It follows the immediate local slope rather than solving every nonlinear transformation ahead.

First-order means that small nearby changes are treated as if their effects combine in a locally simple way. This can work well enough to reveal structure while missing interactions that appear only after larger moves or combinations. The lens is an instrument with a range, not a universal map of the model.

Its output also needs careful language. When the lens ranks a token such as spider, deception, or winter, it has not discovered private prose sitting intact in the activation. It has found a direction whose local downstream influence aligns with that possible word under the averaged map.

That distinction protects both sides of the result. The token label is connected to the model's own output machinery, so it is more than a free association invented by an observer. The connection remains approximate, sparse, and method-dependent, so the label cannot be read as a complete hidden sentence.

Before trusting a new measuring instrument in strange territory, scientists look for cases where the answer is constrained independently. A thermometer can be checked against known fixed points. A lens can be tested on tasks designed to contain an identifiable intermediate.

The J-space researchers constructed prompt distributions where a model needed a known single-token intermediary to reach an answer. The task might require an internal step whose likely identity was specified by the problem design. That gave the researchers something external against which to compare the lens.

Across six such prompt distributions, the Jacobian lens recovered known intermediates better than two comparison lenses used for reading transformer states. Its identified directions also produced larger effects in ablation and swap experiments.

The two tests constrain different failure modes. Readout performance says the instrument finds the expected intermediary. Causal effects say the selected activity participates in producing the measured result. Neither comparison depends only on whether the printed label sounds plausible to a researcher.

One comparison method did better at predicting the model's next-token distribution, which was the objective it had been tuned to serve. The J-lens performed better on the paper's intermediate-recovery objective. An instrument can be superior for one question while another remains superior for a different one.

The intermediates were known in advance and expressed as single tokens. Ordinary reasoning can involve phrases, relationships, ambiguous concepts, or states with no neat word. Performance on the controlled cases licenses further experiments. It does not establish perfect fidelity for open-ended thought.

Known-intermediate validation avoids a circular trap. If researchers inspect an open-ended readout and choose whichever token seems meaningful afterward, almost any fluent label can appear impressive. A task with an independently specified intermediary creates a prediction before the readout is seen.

Ranking gives the prediction a graded form. The expected token can appear near the top, somewhere in the candidate list, or nowhere useful. Comparison methods can be scored under the same rule. The task remains artificial, but the evaluation no longer depends on a compelling anecdote.

Causal comparisons add a second constraint. A direction that names the known intermediary should produce an appropriate effect when patched or ablated. Better labels with weaker intervention effects would tell a different story from better labels with stronger effects. In the reported tests, the Jacobian lens improved both kinds of evidence relative to the comparison lenses on the selected objective.

The paper itself reports places where readouts are hard to interpret. Some early-layer activity remains inaccessible to the method. Multi-token concepts create difficulties. An automated auditing attempt can fail even when interesting information is present. These negative results belong beside the successful benchmark.

A lens can miss something for several reasons. The content may be nonlinear. Its effect may depend on a combination of directions. The averaged map may blur a prompt-specific route. A useful concept may not correspond cleanly to one output token. Absence from the readout therefore cannot establish absence from the model.

The success case is still remarkable. For the first time in this story, a researcher has a principled route from transient hidden activity toward words the model might be able to use in report. The route is validated on constrained intermediates and open to causal testing.

The paper then makes a second move. A dense residual activation can point somewhat toward many possible token directions. The researchers seek a sparse description: a limited set of Jacobian-lens directions whose nonnegative combination approximates the part of the activation they want to study.

Sparse means that relatively few directions are active in the description at one time. Nonnegative means the method builds the component by adding selected directions with positive strengths rather than balancing every direction against a large negative counterpart.

In simplified terms, the method searches for a token direction that explains a useful part of the current activation, accounts for that contribution, and searches again among what remains. The process continues until it reaches its sparse allowance. The result is a short list of active verbalizable directions rather than a vocabulary-sized cloud of tiny scores.

That iterative search is called gradient pursuit in the paper. Its presence is another reminder that J-space is constructed through a procedure. The procedure chooses directions under a constraint. It does not open a hatch and reveal a pre-labelled inventory.

The procedure usually permits roughly twenty-five active directions. That number is a methodological choice, not a discovered law of cognition. A different sparsity setting could divide the activation differently or retain more content.

The paper calls the resulting sparse, token-indexed decomposition J-space. Token-indexed means its candidate directions are tied to possible vocabulary tokens through the J-lens. At each selected point in the model's activity, the method can describe a limited mixture of directions associated with words the model could later verbalize.

A hypothetical winter state might receive directions labelled winter, snow, and cold. Another decomposition could use freezing, season, and weather while covering similar structure. The lists would differ in vocabulary while preserving part of the same operational content.

The labels can also overlap in meaning. Natural language does not provide a perfectly independent coordinate system. Winter and snow are related; deception and manipulation can share contexts; evaluation and test can point toward neighboring concerns. Sparse output is easier to inspect, though it does not make language itself mathematically unique.

The word space can create a misleading picture. J-space is not a small anatomical module tucked inside the network. It is not one fixed set of neurons. It is not a chamber where complete thoughts wait to be observed.

Mathematically, its token directions can span the full residual stream. The sparse decomposition selects a small active mixture for a particular state under a particular method. Another valid decomposition may describe overlapping content differently.

This is why calling J-space low-dimensional can be misleading. Few active directions at one moment does not mean all possible J-space activity lives inside one fixed little plane. Different prompts can recruit different token directions across the larger residual space.

A music analogy catches the relationship. A song may use only a few notes at one instant while the full instrument can produce many notes across time. The sparse chord is limited; the instrument's total range is not reduced to that chord. Neural token directions are neither literal notes nor necessarily independent, but the distinction between sparse activity and a permanently tiny space carries over.

The colour-channel lesson from Chapter Five returns here. A useful direction can be distributed across coordinates. Several directions can overlap. Recovering a sparse basis does not prove that each component is one unique natural concept. It provides an operational vocabulary for experiments.

The room metaphor can still help if it is kept functional. Imagine a meeting room whose whiteboard is available to several teams. Content placed there can be read, modified, and reused across tasks. The room is defined by access and connection, not by the material from which its walls are made.

J-space earns a room-like description only if its content is available to multiple model operations. The sparse readout alone identifies candidate material. Report swaps, instruction-driven modulation, reuse in reasoning, connectivity, and ablation test whether the material behaves like shared working content.

The metaphor adds no occupant. A meeting room in ordinary life contains people who see the board. J-space is an activation regime in a feed-forward transformer. Its functional availability does not imply a witness observing the content.

It also adds no autobiography. The decomposition is calculated from transient activity at token positions and layers during a forward pass. When the request ends, J-space does not become a personal archive. Product memory, retrieved conversation history, current context, and temporary internal representation remain separate mechanisms.

The paper finds that the most workspace-like readouts concentrate in a bounded regime later in the model's layers. Closer to the output, the content becomes more directly aligned with the words being produced. Early-layer absence may reflect the instrument's weakness as well as a real difference in function.

This creates a rough progression rather than a sharp doorway. Earlier activity is transformed through many operations. A later band shows more of the flexible, verbalizable content the authors are seeking. Near the final output, representation increasingly serves the immediate next-token decision.

The boundaries were chosen partly after examining the observed patterns. They are useful descriptions of these models under this analysis, not universal anatomical borders. A new model or a different lens may draw them elsewhere.

That caveat follows from the measurement. The J-lens is built around downstream verbal influence. Early computation may carry information in forms that have not yet become easy to map toward output tokens. A poor readout cannot tell whether the content is missing or merely encoded outside the lens's reach.

Even in the later regime, the sparse component explains only a limited share of the full activation variance. The median reported share remains under one tenth. Most of the numerical activity lies outside this sparse verbalizable description.

Here, variance means how much of the activation's changing numerical pattern the sparse reconstruction accounts for across the measurements. Imagine a delivery sheet whose numbers vary because of addresses, weights, times, barcodes, and route conditions. A few destination annotations might account for little of all that variation while still determining where the parcel goes. Likewise, a low variance share says the J-space description leaves most numerical change unexplained. It does not say the captured part has only a tenth of the causal importance.

Variance is not importance. A small component can guide a consequential decision, while a large component can support routine processing. The measurement does show that J-space is not a summary of everything happening inside the model.

This limited occupancy is part of what makes the result interesting. A comparatively small slice of activity may provide content available for report and flexible use, while a much larger mass of computation proceeds in forms the lens does not capture. That architecture begins to resemble theories in which selected information becomes broadly accessible against a background of specialized processing.

Resemblance is the proper word at this stage. The paper has defined an operational regime and measured where it appears. The stronger functional case depends on experiments still ahead. The human comparison will wait until we understand both systems more carefully.

Scope matters before the striking examples begin. Most of the main evidence comes from Claude Sonnet Four Point Five, with selected corroboration in a few closely related Claude models, including Claude Opus Four Point Five and Claude Opus Four Point Six.

These are a small, closely related proprietary Claude model family from one developer. Results repeated among them are useful. They do not establish that every transformer, every language model family, or every training method develops the same regime.

The models are also proprietary. Outside researchers cannot inspect their weights or repeat every experiment exactly. Anthropic released a public reference implementation under the Apache Two Point Zero license, along with a walkthrough, tests, and synthetic prompt sets for open-weight decoder transformers.

Three proof layers are easy to collapse. Code availability lets another researcher inspect and run the implementation. Method portability asks whether it works on another model. Empirical reproduction asks whether an independent team can recreate the target results under comparable conditions.

The Apache-licensed release establishes code availability and enables portability tests. Early Qwen and Gemma work begins to probe the second layer. Exact reproduction of the proprietary Claude evidence remains unavailable because the public materials do not include the Claude weights and complete production assets.

Early outside work is mixed in the useful sense of that word. An invited external commentator reported successes on several report and modulation tests in a Qwen model, alongside failures or confounds in poetry, arithmetic, and multi-step tasks. A separate practitioner demonstrated parts of the approach on a Gemma model and emphasized technical caveats.

These efforts show that the method can travel beyond Claude in at least some settings. Their uneven results also warn against treating J-space as a universal component already established across model families.

The outside assessments differ philosophically as well. Some researchers regard the functional convergence as important evidence about access-like processing. The neuroscientist Anil Seth accepts the functional result as interesting while rejecting a move from that result to subjective experience under his biological view of consciousness.

His position is an interpretation, not an experimental disproof of machine experience. The disagreement illustrates that two people can accept the same report-and-control findings and assign them different significance because their theories of consciousness differ.

The final research sweep covered the first ten days after publication, through July sixteenth, twenty twenty-six. It found serious commentary, small open-model demonstrations, an application preprint, and partial successes and failures, but no peer-reviewed follow-up or complete independent reproduction. Within that short, index-lagged window, the fair status was promising and early; generality, stability, and exact replication remained open.

Several future observations would move that status. Independent teams could reproduce the known-intermediate advantage on open models. They could test whether sparse directions remain stable across corpora and analysis choices. They could report failures in languages, architectures, or tasks the original study did not cover. Each result would narrow where the instrument works.

Longer scrutiny could also clarify which findings depend on the averaged first-order approximation. A prompt-specific or nonlinear method may recover content the J-lens misses. If so, J-space would remain one useful view of verbalizable activity rather than the final inventory of accessible representation.

This evidence status is more informative than either excitement or dismissal. A new instrument can be useful before it becomes standard. Its readings can change what questions are experimentally available even while its blind spots remain under study.

The limits also make the next results more meaningful, because we know what would count as support. If J-space content can be read but never altered, it may be an observer's convenient summary. If a swap redirects the model's report, the candidate state has causal force under that task. If instructions can summon or suppress content, ordinary model control can reach it. If the same content supports an unspoken intermediate, it can be used before it becomes output.

Selective ablation raises the bar again. If disrupting the sparse regime impairs flexible internal reasoning while leaving much routine prediction intact, the result suggests a functional division. The replacement and compensation cautions from Chapter Six still apply, but the pattern would be stronger than a label alone.

The paper tests each of these possibilities. Its examples include chosen categories, intermediate facts that remain unspoken, rhymes prepared before they are needed, and broad ablations that change some abilities much more than others. Later chapters will take those cases one by one.

For the moment, one retrieval is enough. A hidden state lives along the transformer's forward path. The Jacobian lens estimates which nearby directions would influence possible later words. J-space is the sparse token-indexed description built from those directions. Neither term identifies a literal room or a complete record of the computation.

That leaves the question the instrument was built to answer. When a word appears in the readout, does the model merely contain a decodable trace, or can it read, write, and reuse that content through its own operations?

The first causal demonstration begins with a simple report. A model chooses an item from a category. The researchers alter the candidate internal content. Then they wait to see which answer comes out.
The Room Earns Its Name

The first test is almost modest enough to miss. A model is asked to choose one item from a category and answer with a single word. The category might contain several equally valid possibilities. The output tells the researchers which one the model selected.
Late in the forward pass, the J-lens ranks the chosen item highly in the sparse readout. The model says one word, and a direction associated with that word appears in J-space before the report.

Correlation alone would leave the warning-light problem. The chosen word and the internal direction may share a cause without the direction helping produce the report. Chapter Six gave us the next move: change the candidate state.

The researchers replace the J-space coordinate associated with the chosen item with a coordinate associated with another item from the same category. The model's verbal report shifts toward the inserted item.

Across fourteen category tasks, the combination repeats. Chosen answers rank highly in late J-space readouts, and selected swaps redirect the one-word report toward the alternative. The precise success varies, but the load-bearing fact is causal: changing what appears in the sparse internal description changes what the model says.

The experiment resembles replacing one entry on the whiteboard in our instrumented meeting room. A team preparing the final response reads the altered entry and changes its answer. The intervention does not reveal every route through the organization. It shows that the edited content is connected to the report under this task.

The next experiment begins with a direction already shown to steer the model toward a concept. The researchers treat that steering direction as a concept vector. Their sparse-decomposition method finds the nearest version expressible with a few J-lens directions; that is its J-space component. Subtracting that approximation from the original leaves a remainder outside the measured J-space. They intervene with matched magnitudes so that raw size alone cannot explain the difference.

The small J-space component produces much stronger report effects than the much larger remainder. This concentration supports the claim that the method has isolated something especially connected to verbal report, rather than simply finding whichever intervention injects the most numerical energy.

Magnitude matching closes off one simple explanation. Without it, the J-space intervention might win because it pushes harder. With the size of the interventions controlled, placement in the verbalizable component becomes the important difference.

The remainder is especially revealing because it is not empty. Most of the concept vector's numerical mass can sit outside the sparse J-space component. That larger activity may still serve other functions. It simply has much less power to change the model's report under this protocol.

The experiment therefore separates amount of activity from access to report. A small, well-connected direction can matter more for saying the concept than a larger body of related representation elsewhere. Functional position can outweigh raw size.

The protocol uses an explicit implanted-thought framing when the model is later asked to describe what appeared internally. That framing is useful because it creates a controlled behavioral test. It also limits the conclusion. The result does not validate every ordinary self-report a language model gives about its hidden processing.

An implanted-thought request tells the model what kind of answer is expected. It encourages the model to treat an unusual internal perturbation as something to name. Ordinary conversation lacks that controlled setup. A model describing its preferences, confidence, or reasoning may be responding to many learned conversational cues beyond the J-space component being measured here.

The experiment can still test privileged access in a local sense. The model reports the small inserted component more readily than a matched intervention in the larger remainder. What it cannot do is turn every first-person sentence into verified introspection.

Nor does the swap prove that J-space contains the one true representation of the item. The decomposition is sparse and nonunique. Other activity may carry related information. The finding is narrower and still substantial: the component selected by the J-space method has a concentrated causal relationship with the tested verbal report.

Reportability is one property expected of a shared workspace. A stronger case asks whether task demands can change what becomes available there. A passive display can be read. A working surface can also be updated according to the job.

The researchers created an unusual control. They asked models to copy unrelated text while also instructing them to focus on a target concept or to ignore it. The visible text being copied could remain the same. The instruction changed what the J-lens found in J-space.

This separation is valuable. If the target word appeared only because the model printed or repeated it, surface copying could explain the readout. Instead, the model could maintain the copying behavior while target-related internal content rose or fell according to a second instruction.
Task-directed increase or suppression of selected content is called modulation. The term describes a measured change in availability. It does not require a conscious executive deciding what to admit.

Focus instructions generally increased the target's presence. Ignore instructions generally reduced it. The suppression was incomplete, and exact performance depended heavily on prompt wording and task.

The imperfection reveals something important about language control. Telling a system not to think about a target requires mentioning the target. Mention and prohibition can prime the very content they are meant to suppress. The familiar human joke about avoiding a white bear has a structural cousin here without establishing that the mechanisms or experiences are the same.

The model has to process the forbidden concept to understand the instruction. That processing can leave a J-space trace even when later behavior tries to avoid it. A different phrasing may create a weaker or stronger effect. Directed modulation is real in the experiments and far from reliable thought control.

Silent arithmetic provides another case. While visible output remains occupied with an unrelated copying task, an instruction can cause an arithmetic intermediate to become readable in J-space. The calculation-related content does not need to appear in the copied text.
This does not show that arithmetic always occurs in J-space. It shows that selected task-relevant intermediate content can be recruited there while the surface channel is doing something else.

An implicit task contrast sharpens the point. A property can be useful to the model without its verbal label becoming prominent. When the task requires naming the property, the label becomes more explicit in J-space. The same underlying information can be used narrowly or made available for report depending on what the task demands.

Imagine a system sorting objects by whether they can float. It may use density-related patterns to choose correctly without foregrounding the word buoyancy. If the task changes from sorting to explaining the sorting rule, a verbal label becomes useful. The example is illustrative, but it captures the tested relationship: naming demand changes which form of the information becomes explicit.

This helps with a subtle misconception. Information does not have to appear in J-space before it can influence anything. A property may guide a specialized operation outside the sparse readout. J-space becomes especially relevant when that property must be named, combined with other content, or carried into a different downstream use.

The difference is similar to recognizing a familiar route while driving and giving spoken directions to someone else. Successful navigation can rely on cues that are difficult to verbalize. Explaining the route requires selecting landmarks and making them available to speech. The human mechanisms are different; the relationship between use and report is the point.

That difference begins to separate information from access. A system may respond correctly to a property through specialized processing. A naming request requires a form connected to verbal output. J-space grows more visible when that broader availability is useful.

The meeting-room analogy now gains a second relationship. Content can be written to the shared surface according to the current assignment. Some material becomes prominent; some is suppressed; some remains faint because the instruction itself keeps it active.

There is still no tiny manager choosing the agenda. Prompt tokens alter attention, residual updates, and later computation through learned mechanisms. Modulation names the resulting control relationship. It avoids adding an executive homunculus to explain the executive-like behavior.

The first two experiments concern report. A category choice moves into speech. An instruction changes which content is poised for speech. The next experiment asks whether J-space can carry a step that does useful work without ever being printed.

Consider a two-hop problem. The model has to identify that the relevant creature is a spider, retrieve that a spider has eight legs, and use that number to reach a final conclusion. The prompt need not contain the word eight. The answer need not print it.

The two hops can be heard as a small dependency chain. First comes the entity. Spider. Then comes the property needed for the task. Eight legs. The final operation uses eight while producing an answer that may contain neither spider nor eight.

A surface trace would show only the prompt and the final word. The intermediate readout adds a candidate bridge. The patch tests whether traffic actually crosses it. When replacing the eight-related coordinate redirects the conclusion, the bridge has carried causal traffic in that run.

The J-lens surfaces the intermediate direction linking spider to eight. The researchers then swap that coordinate for a different candidate and observe whether the downstream conclusion follows the inserted intermediate.

When the answer changes in the predicted direction, the unspoken step has causal force. Eight is not merely a retrospective label placed on activity that happened to accompany the solution. Under the selected prompt, model, location, swap, and output measure, it mediates the route to the conclusion.

Mediation means that a state sits along a causal path from input to result. It need not be the only path. A highway can carry much of the traffic between two towns while local roads remain. Successful intervention identifies a working route, not a monopoly.

The paper tested a collection of two-hop prompts across three Claude models. Swap success was incomplete but substantial, with stronger reported top-choice results in two of the larger models than in the smaller one. The pattern matters more for listening than the percentages: many swaps redirected the conclusion, and many did not.

The failures keep the example grounded. Some intermediates may be distributed across several token directions. Some tasks may rely on nonverbalizable activity. The J-lens may miss the relevant encoding. The chosen swap may fit badly with the surrounding state. A single-token intermediary is a favorable case for the method.

The result therefore complicates a surface-only account of model reasoning. The output token is not always selected directly from prompt wording. An absent intermediate can form in transient activity, influence later computation, and remain absent from the final prose.

This matters for auditing as well as philosophy. A model can arrive at a conclusion through an internal association the user never sees. If an instrument can surface and perturb that association, a reviewer gains evidence unavailable from input and output alone. The same caution applies: the lens reveals selected verbalizable intermediates, not every hidden cause.

It does not establish that every reasoning step is available to language. The method itself has blind spots, and the next chapter will examine computation that appears to continue outside J-space. The spider case shows possibility and causal use within a selected domain.

This is the right place to retrieve the intervention standard. A decoder finds eight. A swap changes the answer. The licensed conclusion is causal relevance for the tested route. The unlicensed conclusion is that all models always reason through a hidden chain of single-word thoughts.
An unprinted intermediate can also guide a more extended plan. The paper's poetry experiment makes this visible through rhyme.

In one constrained rhyming-couplet task, a line-final rhyme appears in the J-space readout before the second line has been written. The planned rhyme is fight. Words earlier in the line are selected in a way that can lead naturally toward it.

The researchers replace the fight direction with light. The change affects an earlier word choice as well as the completed line. The inserted ending reaches backward in the plan, in the functional sense that future-word preparation changes which current word is generated.

This is a useful form of planning because it produces a prediction about order. The rhyme direction should appear before the final word. It should influence choices that help the line arrive there. A label discovered only after the rhyme was emitted would be much easier to explain as residue.

Changing fight to light creates a second prediction. Earlier words should adjust so that the altered ending remains coherent with the developing line. The reported intervention does change both the path and the ending, strengthening the case that the rhyme direction participates in advance preparation.

Time inside an autoregressive model makes this less paradoxical than it sounds. The model does not revise a word already emitted. Before producing the next word, its current activation can carry information about a desired later ending. That information influences the current choice. Each generated token then becomes part of the context for the step after it.

The rhyme is a future constraint represented during present generation. A human poet may also choose an ending and shape a line toward it. The similarity lies in advance constraint, not in authorship, emotion, or creative experience.

A larger set of constrained poetry prompts tested whether planned rhyme words could be recovered in advance. The results support planning within that task family. They do not establish a general theory of literary intention or show that every good line begins with a hidden final word.

Spider to eight and fight to light reveal two forms of unspoken use. One intermediate supports a factual conclusion. The other supports a sequence of word choices organized around a later constraint. J-space is beginning to look less like an output preview and more like a limited working route.
There is another way to carry an intermediate. The model can print it.

When a language model generates an explicit sequence of intermediate reasoning, the text is often called chain-of-thought. Once emitted, those words become part of the context available to later generation. The model can attend to its own printed arithmetic or premise as it produces the next token.

This changes where useful information lives. A direct-answer request may require a transient intermediate to survive inside activation until it has done its job. An explicit scratchpad places some of the information into text, where later steps can read it from context.

The J-space paper compared mathematical answers produced directly with answers produced through explicit intermediate text. Under J-space ablation, the chain-of-thought condition was more robust than the direct-answer condition on the tested benchmark.

The measured fact is differential robustness. The authors interpret it as evidence that an external scratchpad reduces demand on the internal workspace. Written steps can preserve information that the disrupted J-space route would otherwise need to carry.

Suppose a calculation requires a subtotal and then a final adjustment. In a direct answer, the subtotal may have to remain available as transient internal activity. In a written chain, the subtotal becomes a token sequence. Even if one internal route is weakened later, attention can recover the value from the text.

The scratchpad is external relative to the hidden activation, though it remains inside the model's current input sequence. It is visible to a user and architecturally available to later token generation. That dual status makes generated text a bridge between internal computation and persistent artifacts.

The advantage has a cost. Printing every intermediate uses time and context space. An erroneous step can anchor later generation. Sensitive reasoning may become visible. Externalization changes the computational arrangement; it does not simply reveal an unchanged inner process.

The interpretation fits the architecture. An emitted intermediate becomes an earlier token in the same bounded request. Attention can draw on it during later steps. This is context, the workbench from Chapter Three, not persistent memory in an archive.

If a model writes a partial sum and uses it two tokens later, no cross-session storage is required. The text persists because it remains in the current context window. A new request would need that text supplied again through conversation history, retrieval, or another product mechanism.

The experiment does not establish that generated chain-of-thought is a faithful or complete transcript of internal processing. It establishes a narrower point: emitted text can remain in context and reduce dependence on the ablated J-space route for this task.

The ablation result does not make written reasoning private introspection made public. It shows that generated text can perform a scratchpad function. Some work moves into an externally available medium. Other computation remains in activations, parameters, and pathways the text may not describe.

Here, external state reduces what must remain available in one transient route. That result does not by itself establish a general capability claim for agents, tools, or written notes in other systems.

A workspace therefore need not contain everything used by the system. Some information can be supplied in prompt context. Some can be encoded in durable parameters. Some can be written to an external scratchpad. Some can pass through narrower automatic routes. J-space describes one transient regime among these.
Report, modulation, mediation, and planning make a strong sequence. A final functional question asks whether the same internal content can serve more than one downstream operation.

Suppose a country-related coordinate is changed. One task may ask for the country's capital. Another may ask for its language, currency, or a relation to a neighboring country. If the same inserted coordinate redirects several different operations appropriately, the content is being reused rather than wired to one fixed response.
Reuse across different operations is called flexible use. It is a stronger workspace property than reportability because the state can guide more than one kind of result.

Reportability could arise from a dedicated route leading straight from one representation to one word. Flexible use asks whether the same content can feed several routes. A country direction that changes a capital answer, a language answer, and another country-related operation is behaving more like shared input than a single-output switch.

The paper finds real flexible reuse and substantial unevenness. The same swapped country coordinate redirects several downstream functions in a systematic test. Higher intervention strength raises the total number of successful redirections. Categories differ sharply.

At normal intervention strength, the reported test succeeded on seventy-six of one hundred ninety-two trials. Doubling the strength raised success to one hundred one. Those totals show an effect without approaching universal reliability.

The stronger intervention also needs restraint. A larger push may overcome competing state, and it may create a less natural activation. Improvement with strength supports sensitivity while increasing concern about off-manifold effects. The ordinary-strength result remains an important anchor.

Some combinations fail entirely under the ordinary intervention. Number-relation swaps, in particular, do not support the broad claim that any concept can feed any operation. The workspace resembles a shared medium with learned routes and compatibility constraints, not a universal plug that fits every socket.

Failure by category helps rule out a generic disruption story. If every inserted coordinate merely confused the model, success would not track compatible concept-operation pairs. The uneven pattern suggests structured connectivity, though the precise structure remains incompletely mapped.
